proposals.title: Quantifying the Impact of Non-Stationary Rupture Processes on Regional Ground Motions and Site Response
proposals.motivation: Current ground-motion models and site-response analyses often assume a stationary, point-source representation of earthquakes, which may not capture the complex spatio-temporal evolution of rupture. This simplification can lead to inaccuracies in predicting ground motions, particularly for large events and across regions with complex site conditions like the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta. A more realistic, non-stationary source model is needed to improve hazard assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly modeling the non-stationary evolution of rupture processes—including variations in slip rate, rupture velocity, and stress drop across the fault—will significantly improve the prediction of ground-motion intensity measures and explain observed spatial variability in site amplification.
proposals.method: We will develop a modular framework using the DesignSafe-CI cyberinfrastructure. The method integrates kinematic rupture models with variable slip functions into established ground-motion simulation codes (e.g., Graves and Pitarka). We will generate suites of synthetic ground motions for historical and scenario events in California, systematically varying source non-stationarity parameters. These simulations will be processed using a standardized high-pass filtering tool to ensure consistency. The resulting ground motions will be compared against the NGA-West2 database and region-specific Vs30 data to quantify improvements in fit.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new, open-source framework for incorporating non-stationary source physics into probabilistic seismic hazard analysis (PSHA). It will deliver quantitative corrections to ground-motion prediction equations (GMPEs) and site amplification factors, directly improving engineering design criteria for critical infrastructure in regions with soft soils.
proposals.novelty: While non-stationary source models exist in seismology, their systematic integration into regional-scale ground-motion prediction and site-response practice, using open science platforms like DesignSafe, represents a significant advance beyond the stationary-source assumptions prevalent in pre-2026 engineering seismology.

proposals.title: A Path-Specific Attenuation Model for Large Rupture Distances (>200 km) Incorporating 3D Crustal and Basin Effects
proposals.motivation: Current ground motion models (GMMs) often inadequately capture the complex attenuation and amplification observed at distances beyond 200 km, where seismic waves traverse heterogeneous crustal structures and deep sedimentary basins. This limitation is critical for seismic hazard assessment in stable continental regions and for forecasting shaking from large, distant earthquakes in populated areas like California's Central Valley. Improved path-effect characterization is essential for more accurate long-period ground motion prediction, which drives the risk to tall buildings and long-period infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a GMM component parameterized by region-specific 3D crustal velocity models and basin depth metrics will significantly reduce the aleatory variability (sigma) and bias in predicting ground motions at large rupture distances, compared to models using only simple geometric spreading and anelastic attenuation terms.
proposals.method: We will leverage the extensive ground-motion database and cyberinfrastructure of DesignSafe to compile a global dataset of recordings at Rrup > 200 km. Path-specific metrics will be computed using pre-2026 3D velocity models (e.g., CVM-S4.26, USGS National Crustal Model) to extract variables such as cumulative travel time through low-velocity basins, crustal thickness along the path, and angle of incidence into deep sedimentary regions like the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta. A mixed-effects regression framework will be used to isolate the path term, and its correlation with the 3D metrics will be modeled using both parametric functions and non-parametric machine learning (e.g., gradient-boosted trees) to capture complex interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a modular, physics-informed path-effect component that can be integrated into existing GMMs, directly improving long-distance ground motion prediction for the Next Generation Attenuation (NGA) projects. It will provide a quantitative framework for moving beyond region-based adjustments to path-based adjustments, reducing epistemic uncertainty in hazard models for central and eastern North America and other stable continental regions.
proposals.novelty: While past studies have identified regional differences, this approach systematically quantifies path complexity using readily available 3D Earth models as predictive variables, moving from a categorical 'regionalization' to a continuous, path-specific correction grounded in crustal seismology.

proposals.title: Integrating Multi-Fault Rupture Directivity into Regional Ground-Motion Models for the San Francisco Bay Area
proposals.motivation: Current regional Ground Motion Models (GMMs) used for seismic hazard analysis often treat earthquakes as point sources or simple planar ruptures, inadequately capturing the complex directivity effects of multi-segment ruptures. This is a critical limitation in regions like the San Francisco Bay Area, where complex fault systems (e.g., the Hayward-Rodgers Creek connection) can produce multi-fault ruptures with strong, spatially variable directivity pulses. Ignoring these effects leads to inaccurate ground motion predictions for critical infrastructure and site-specific hazard assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a physics-based parameterization of multi-fault rupture directivity, integrated into a regional GMM framework, will significantly improve the prediction of ground motions, particularly for long-period spectral accelerations, compared to models that use only simple geometric source metrics.
proposals.method: We will leverage the NGA-West2 database and the DesignSafe cyberinfrastructure to access and process ground-motion records. For selected multi-fault scenarios (e.g., Hayward-Rodgers Creek), we will compute directivity parameters (e.g., isochrone-derived quantities like `cDPP`) using kinematic rupture models. These parameters will be incorporated as predictor variables into a regional GMM developed via mixed-effects regression, building upon the functional form of models like the Boore et al. (2014) NGA-West2 GMM. Site effects will be constrained using Vs30 and region-specific near-surface shear-wave velocity profiles from our prior work in the Delta region. The high-pass corner-frequency review tool will ensure consistent record processing.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a practical, open-source GMM adjustment for directivity in multi-fault ruptures, directly usable in probabilistic seismic hazard analysis (PSHA). It will provide a quantitative framework for understanding how complex fault geometry and rupture propagation control regional shaking patterns, moving beyond single-fault paradigms.
proposals.novelty: While directivity models exist for single faults, the systematic integration of multi-fault rupture path and directivity into an empirical GMM calibrated for a specific, complex tectonic region is novel. This bridges the gap between complex physics-based scenario simulations and empirically constrained regional hazard models.

proposals.title: A Basin-Depth-Scaled Ground Motion Model for the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta: Integrating Vs30 and Z2.5 from a Regional Database
proposals.motivation: Current Ground Motion Models (GMMs) primarily use Vs30 as a site proxy, which inadequately captures the frequency-dependent amplification effects of deep sedimentary basins, a key factor in regions like the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta. This omission leads to significant uncertainty in seismic hazard assessment for critical infrastructure in these areas. The 2015 NGA-West2 project highlighted basin depth (Z2.5) as a critical parameter, but region-specific models incorporating local basin geometry are still needed.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly incorporating a basin-depth parameter (Z2.5) alongside Vs30 into a regionally calibrated GMM will significantly improve the prediction of long-period ground motions in the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta, reducing model residuals compared to models using Vs30 alone.
proposals.method: We will compile a region-specific ground-motion database from DesignSafe, incorporating events from the NGA-West2 and relevant regional networks. Using the high-pass corner-frequency review tool from recent work, we will ensure consistent processing. The method involves developing a mixed-effects regression model where the attenuation function is augmented with a basin amplification term scaled by Z2.5. Z2.5 estimates will be derived from a synthesis of the regional Vs30 database and empirical correlations to sediment thickness, creating a 3D basin model for the Delta region.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly available, regionally tailored GMM that explicitly quantifies basin amplification for the Delta, directly applicable to site-specific seismic hazard analysis. It will provide a framework and open dataset on DesignSafe for integrating basin-depth metrics into practice, advancing the goal of physics-informed empirical ground-motion prediction.
proposals.novelty: While basin terms exist in global models, this proposal uniquely focuses on the complex, peaty soils of the Delta, integrating a high-resolution regional Z2.5 model derived from the researcher's existing Vs30 database into a fully probabilistic GMM, rather than applying a generic correction.

proposals.title: Spatial Variability of Spectral and Duration-Based Ground Motion Intensity Measures: A Residual Analysis for the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta Region
proposals.motivation: Ground motion prediction equations (GMPEs) and site response analyses have traditionally focused on peak and spectral acceleration (PGA, PSA). However, structural and geotechnical performance can be sensitive to other intensity measures (IMs), such as significant duration (Ds) and inelastic spectral displacement (Sdi). The spatial variability of these non-standard IMs, particularly in complex, soft-soil regions like the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta, is poorly characterized, limiting the accuracy of seismic hazard and risk assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial correlation structure of ground motion residuals—capturing systematic regional deviations from GMPEs—differs significantly for duration-based and inelastic spectral IMs compared to standard elastic IMs, and this structure is strongly influenced by the unique, spatially variable soil conditions of the Delta region.
proposals.method: We will leverage the DesignSafe cyberinfrastructure and the NGA-West2 database to access processed ground-motion records. Using a high-pass corner-frequency review tool consistent with Brandenberg's prior work, we will compute a suite of IMs (including Ds5-75, Ds5-95, and Sdi for various periods) for events in the California region. We will compute total residuals for each IM relative to a robust GMPE, partition them into between-event and within-event components, and perform geostatistical analysis (e.g., variogram modeling) on the within-event residuals. The analysis will explicitly incorporate high-resolution near-surface Vs30 and soil profile data from the Delta region to test for correlation with the observed spatial patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first published models for the spatial correlation of duration and inelastic displacement IMs, directly applicable to regional-scale seismic risk and infrastructure resilience studies. It will provide critical guidance on whether current practice of using spatial correlation models derived from PGA/PSA is adequate for vector-based probabilistic seismic hazard analysis (PSHA) and ground motion selection for performance-based engineering.
proposals.novelty: While residual analysis for PGA and PSA is established, a systematic investigation of spatial correlation for duration and inelastic IMs, explicitly tied to the effects of spatially variable Delta soils, represents a significant expansion of the state-of-the-art pre-2026.

proposals.title: Imaging 3D Crustal Heterogeneity in the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta: A Tomographic Framework for Path-Specific Ground-Motion Prediction
proposals.motivation: Ground-motion models (GMMs) rely on simplified 1D path-effect terms, which fail to capture the complex 3D velocity heterogeneity of sedimentary basins and fault zones, leading to significant epistemic uncertainty in seismic hazard analysis. The Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta region, with its deep, irregular sedimentary deposits, exemplifies an area where these path effects are poorly constrained, as highlighted by prior near-surface Vs studies. This project directly addresses the need to move beyond generic attenuation functions to physics-based, path-specific corrections.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a 3D seismic velocity model of the upper crust, derived from ambient noise and earthquake tomography, can be used to develop deterministic, frequency-dependent path corrections that significantly reduce the residuals of existing GMMs for events recorded in the Delta region.
proposals.method: We will leverage the extensive waveform database from regional networks (e.g., NCEDC, SCEDC) and the DesignSafe cyberinfrastructure for data management. The method involves a two-step seismic tomography approach: (1) ambient noise tomography to construct a high-resolution shallow crustal Vs model, and (2) double-difference earthquake tomography using curated events (processed with a standardized high-pass corner-frequency tool) to image deeper Vp and Vs structures. The 3D model will then be used in finite-difference wave propagation simulations to compute path-specific amplification/attenuation operators for integration into GMMs.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first publicly available, geophysically constrained 3D velocity model of the Delta crust, directly informing regional seismic hazard assessments. It will deliver a modular framework for deriving path corrections from tomography, moving the field towards more deterministic ground-motion prediction. The results and workflows will be shared openly via DesignSafe, enhancing reproducible science in natural hazards.
proposals.novelty: While tomography is well-established, its direct application to generate frequency-dependent path terms for region-specific GMM calibration in a complex sedimentary basin is novel. This integrates Brandenberg's expertise in site response and ground-motion processing with advanced imaging to target a critical gap in path-effect parameterization.

proposals.title: Spatial Random Forest Modeling of Ground-Motion Model Residuals to Uncover Systematic Site and Path Effects in the California Central Valley
proposals.motivation: Ground-motion models (GMMs) exhibit significant spatial correlation in their residuals, indicating systematic, unmodeled effects related to complex site response and wave propagation. These patterns, evident in databases like NGA-West2, lead to biased hazard estimates, particularly in regions with deep sedimentary basins and variable near-surface geology like the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta. Identifying and predicting these patterns is crucial for improving site-specific seismic hazard analysis and engineering design.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning models, trained on spatially referenced residuals and high-resolution geospatial features (e.g., Vs30, basin depth, topographic gradient), can identify and predict the spatial structure of GMM residuals, revealing systematic biases attributable to specific site and path characteristics not captured by conventional ergodic models.
proposals.method: We will compile a spatial database of GMM residuals from recorded ground motions in California, leveraging platforms like DesignSafe for data access. Geospatial features (Vs30 from community models, depth to 1.0 km/s shear-wave velocity, terrain slope, geologic unit) will be assembled for each recording site. A Random Forest model with spatial cross-validation will be trained to predict residuals, with feature importance analysis identifying dominant controls. Spatial patterns in model predictions will be mapped and compared to known geologic structures and previously observed regional attenuation effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a interpretable, data-driven framework for quantifying and visualizing non-ergodic ground-motion effects at a regional scale. It will directly contribute to the development of partially non-ergodic GMMs and provide actionable insights for refining site response corrections in seismic hazard models for complex regions.
proposals.novelty: While spatial correlation of residuals is well-known, this approach applies geospatially-aware machine learning not just to model correlation structure, but to explicitly predict residuals using interpretable physical features, moving beyond descriptive kriging to a diagnostic and predictive framework.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Physics-Based and Empirical Framework for Basin-Specific Ground Motion Prediction in the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta
proposals.motivation: Current engineering ground motion models (GMMs) rely on simplified proxies like Vs30 to capture site effects, often inadequately representing the complex 3D wave amplification and resonance of deep sedimentary basins. This limitation introduces significant uncertainty in seismic hazard assessment for critical infrastructure in basin regions like California's Delta. Integrating advanced basin response simulations from seismology into engineering practice is essential for more accurate and physically realistic ground motion prediction.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating 3D basin response Green's functions, derived from regional velocity models and validated against recorded ground motions, into a hybrid empirical-physics-based GMM framework will significantly reduce the epistemic uncertainty in predicting long-period spectral accelerations for sites within deep sedimentary basins.
proposals.method: We will leverage the 3D Community Velocity Model for California (CVM-S4.26) to compute basin response functions using the SW4 finite-difference wave propagation code for a suite of scenario earthquakes. These deterministic amplification factors will be convolved with empirical host-to-rock GMMs (e.g., NGA-West2) to generate hybrid predictions. The framework will be validated and calibrated using the extensive ground-motion database on DesignSafe, focusing on events in the Delta region, with careful processing using established high-pass corner-frequency selection tools to ensure data quality.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a practical, open-source methodology for generating basin-adjusted ground motions, directly usable in engineering practice and probabilistic seismic hazard analysis (PSHA). It will deliver a calibrated, region-specific model for the Delta, quantifying the reduction in uncertainty achieved by moving beyond Vs30. The resulting workflow and datasets will be shared via DesignSafe to enable open science and community validation.
proposals.novelty: While 3D basin modeling is common in seismology, its direct integration into a scalable, empirical GMM framework for engineering application is a significant advancement. The novelty lies in the systematic convolution of deterministic basin Green's functions with empirical host-to-rock models to create a hybrid tool that is both physically rigorous and practically implementable for hazard analysis.

proposals.title: A Continental-Scale Path Attenuation Model for North America Integrating Crustal Structure and Anelastic Effects from Global Datasets
proposals.motivation: Current ground-motion models (GMMs) for seismic hazard analysis often rely on simplified path terms, such as geometric spreading and a frequency-independent quality factor (Q), which may not capture complex, frequency-dependent anelastic attenuation across diverse continental-scale tectonic regimes. This limitation introduces significant uncertainty in predicting ground motions for critical long-period infrastructure and for earthquakes with paths traversing multiple geologic provinces. A physics-informed, data-driven continental-scale path model is needed to improve the accuracy of probabilistic seismic hazard analysis (PSHA) at regional and national scales.
proposals.hypothesis: That a path attenuation model for North America, calibrated on a global database of processed ground motions and constrained by 3D crustal and upper mantle velocity and attenuation (Q) structure, will significantly reduce the epistemic uncertainty in ground-motion prediction for distances beyond 200 km compared to existing empirical GMMs.
proposals.method: We will leverage the global NGA-West2 and RESORCE-ISEP databases, processed using standardized high-pass corner-frequency selection tools, to extract empirical path terms. These terms will be regressed against a 3D path parameterization derived from pre-2026 continental-scale models (e.g., CRUST1.0, LITHO1.0) and global Q models (e.g., QRFSI). The core technical approach will implement a modular, region-aware attenuation framework within the DesignSafe cyberinfrastructure, using a Bayesian hierarchical model to partition source, path, and site effects while explicitly incorporating path-integrated metrics of crustal thickness, average Vs, and anelastic attenuation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first open, continent-scale path attenuation model for North America that is directly tied to geophysical structure, providing a transparent alternative to purely empirical GMM extrapolation. It will deliver a publicly accessible database of path-specific correction factors and associated uncertainties, directly usable within the DesignSafe ecosystem to refine seismic hazard maps and ground-motion simulations for large-scale engineering projects.
proposals.novelty: While regional path effects have been studied, this proposal integrates a massive global ground-motion database with 3D geophysical models at a continental scale using a hierarchical Bayesian framework, moving beyond single-region calibrations to a unified, physically constrained model for complex trans-crustal wave propagation.

proposals.title: Inverting High-Frequency Ground-Motion Amplification for 3D Basin Depth in the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta Using a Dense Regional Database
proposals.motivation: Accurate basin depth models are critical for predicting site-specific earthquake ground shaking, yet many regions, like California's Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta, lack high-resolution subsurface data. Current mapping relies on sparse geophysical surveys, leaving significant uncertainty in seismic hazard models. This project addresses this gap by leveraging the dense ground-motion recordings from recent seismic events to empirically constrain basin geometry where direct measurements are unavailable.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial patterns of high-frequency (>1 Hz) ground-motion amplification, derived from a large database of recorded events, can be inverted to produce a reliable, spatially continuous map of sediment-basement depth. This amplification signal is primarily controlled by the impedance contrast at the basin base and is less sensitive to small-scale near-surface heterogeneity.
proposals.method: We will utilize the extensive ground-motion database from the DesignSafe-CI platform, focusing on events recorded by regional networks in Northern California. For each recording station, we will compute the high-frequency amplification factor (HFA) as the ratio of observed spectral acceleration to a regionally attenuated bedrock reference motion, following methodologies from recent high-pass filter selection studies. A grid-based inversion algorithm will be implemented to iteratively adjust a 3D basin depth model until the predicted HFA pattern—calculated using a simplified 1D resonance model—best fits the observed amplification database. The inversion will be constrained by existing Vs30 and shear-wave velocity profile data from the researcher's prior work in the Delta.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, observationally derived basin depth model for a critical region of California, directly informing the next generation of physics-based seismic hazard simulations. It will establish a reproducible, data-driven framework for mapping subsurface geometry in other poorly characterized sedimentary basins worldwide, enhancing the accuracy of ground-motion predictions.
proposals.novelty: While amplification is commonly used to estimate site effects (Vs30), directly inverting it for 3D basin depth using a large-scale empirical ground-motion database represents a significant methodological advance beyond traditional geophysical interpolation. This approach uniquely leverages the dense spatial sampling of seismic events as an imaging source.

proposals.title: Calibration of Advanced Constitutive Models for Large-Deformation Soil-Structure Interaction Using Regional Ground-Motion Databases and High-Fidelity Site Data
proposals.motivation: Current numerical models for soil-structure interaction (SSI) often fail to accurately predict system performance under extreme loading, such as from large-magnitude earthquakes, where soils experience large deformations and near-failure conditions. This limitation hinders the resilience-based design of critical infrastructure in regions like the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta, where soft soils amplify seismic hazards. Enhancing these constitutive models is essential for reducing uncertainties in performance-based earthquake engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: Constitutive models for soft soils (e.g., advanced plasticity or SANISAND frameworks) can be significantly refined for large-deformation SSI by calibrating their parameters against a curated database of recorded ground motions and associated high-resolution site characterization data, leading to more accurate predictions of structural response extremes.
proposals.method: We will leverage the DesignSafe-CI cyberinfrastructure to access and process a regional database of ground-motion recordings, applying a standardized high-pass filtering protocol informed by our prior work on corner-frequency selection. Site-specific shear wave velocity (Vs) profiles, extending our Delta region analyses, will provide the foundational geotechnical input. The core technical approach involves implementing a Bayesian updating framework to calibrate constitutive model parameters (e.g., for PM4Silt or a modified Drucker-Prager model) by inverting recorded soil and structural response from well-instrumented case histories. Numerical simulations will be conducted using open-source finite element platforms (e.g., OpenSees) capable of modeling large deformations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a set of validated, regionally-tuned constitutive model parameters for soft soils under extreme SSI conditions, directly usable by practitioners. It will also produce a publicly accessible database on DesignSafe linking processed ground motions, site data, and calibrated model inputs, enhancing reproducibility and open science in geotechnical earthquake engineering.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic fusion of region-specific ground-motion databases, high-resolution site characterization, and Bayesian inversion specifically targeted at calibrating constitutive models for the large-deformation and near-failure regimes critical for extreme SSI, a step beyond common calibration for small-strain response.

proposals.title: Modeling Time-Varying Ground-Motion Intensity Using Econometric Volatility Models: A GARCH Framework for Accelerogram Analysis
proposals.motivation: Current ground-motion characterization relies on stationary measures like peak ground acceleration (PGA) and spectral ordinates, which smooth over the critical time-varying intensity within an accelerogram. This temporal evolution of shaking intensity, or 'volatility,' is crucial for understanding structural response and damage potential but lacks a robust quantitative framework. Econometrics has long addressed similar volatility clustering in financial time-series, offering mature tools directly applicable to this unsolved problem in earthquake engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: The time-varying volatility within strong ground-motion accelerograms exhibits autoregressive conditional heteroskedasticity (ARCH) effects, meaning past shaking intensity influences current volatility. Therefore, Generalized ARCH (GARCH) models can parsimoniously characterize this non-stationary intensity, with model parameters correlating with source, path, and site effects.
proposals.method: We will apply univariate GARCH(1,1) and EGARCH models to the squared residuals of high-pass filtered acceleration time-series from the NGA-West2 database and region-specific datasets like those for the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta. The analysis will use a systematic high-pass corner-frequency selection tool, akin to Brandenberg's prior work, to ensure proper baseline correction. Model fitting will be performed via maximum likelihood estimation, and the resulting volatility time-series and model parameters (e.g., persistence, leverage effects) will be analyzed against earthquake magnitude, distance, and VS30.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new quantitative metric—the conditional volatility time-series—for ground-motion records, moving beyond scalar intensity measures. It will establish a direct methodological bridge between econometrics and seismology, enabling new correlations between volatility parameters and geophysical variables. The outcomes could inform more physically realistic stochastic ground-motion models and improve risk assessments by better capturing the temporal structure of intense shaking.
proposals.novelty: While GARCH models are standard in finance, their application to characterize the intrinsic, physics-driven volatility within individual ground-motion accelerograms is novel. This approach fundamentally re-frames the accelerogram not as a stationary process but as a conditionally heteroskedastic one, a perspective not explored in mainstream ground-motion modeling before 2026.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Impact of Correlated Seismic and High-Water-Level Demands on Levee Performance in the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta
proposals.motivation: Current probabilistic seismic hazard analysis (PSHA) and flood hazard assessments for critical infrastructure like levees typically treat seismic shaking and high-water-level demands as independent. This assumption is non-conservative for regions like the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta, where storm surges or king tides can coincide with seismic events, significantly increasing the risk of liquefaction and levee failure. A rigorous, data-driven assessment of this correlation is essential for accurate risk-informed design and resilience planning.
proposals.hypothesis: The joint probability of coincident high seismic intensity measures (e.g., peak ground acceleration) and extreme water levels in the Delta region is significantly greater than the product of their marginal probabilities, and this correlation leads to a substantial increase in computed levee fragility compared to the assumption of independence.
proposals.method: We will leverage the DesignSafe-CI cyberinfrastructure to access and process regional databases of ground motions (e.g., NGA-West2) and tidal/storm-surge records. A copula-based statistical framework will be employed to model the dependence structure between seismic intensity measures (PGA, Sa(T)) and water levels, using historical and synthetic event catalogs. Site-specific ground response analyses, informed by Brandenberg's prior Vs work in the Delta, will be performed for correlated hazard scenarios to compute dynamic pore pressures and factor of safety against liquefaction for representative levee sections.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first region-specific, quantitative model for correlated seismic and high-water hazards for the California Delta. It will deliver a practical framework and open-source tools for performing multi-hazard levee fragility analysis, directly informing updates to engineering guidelines and risk models used by state agencies like the Department of Water Resources.
proposals.novelty: While multi-hazard risk is recognized, this proposal moves beyond qualitative discussion by quantitatively inverting the standard independence assumption in PSHA for a specific, critical infrastructure problem. It uniquely integrates copula methods with site-specific ground response and liquefaction analysis tailored to the complex soils of the Delta.

proposals.title: Assessing the Regional Transferability of a Japanese Seismic Collapse Capacity Model Using Ecological Niche Concepts and Multi-Database Validation
proposals.motivation: Seismic fragility and capacity models are often developed from region-specific case history databases, such as those from Japan, creating uncertainty in their global application. The lack of a formal, quantitative framework for testing model transferability to new tectonic and built environments hinders the development of reliable risk models for regions with sparse empirical data. This research directly addresses the critical need for validated, portable seismic performance prediction tools.
proposals.hypothesis: A seismic collapse capacity model calibrated on Japanese building data will maintain predictive accuracy when applied to case histories from other regions, provided those cases occupy a comparable multi-dimensional parameter space (or 'niche') defined by key structural and ground motion variables.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Ecological Niche Modeling (ENM) validation paradigm. First, we define the model's calibration 'niche' in a multi-dimensional space of parameters (e.g., Sa(T1), Vs30, building material, height, age) using the Japanese database. Using the DesignSafe platform and other resources (e.g., PEER NGA-West2 database, European datasets), we will extract global case histories. We will employ a multivariate matching algorithm (e.g., Mahalanobis distance) to identify global subsets that occupy the Japanese model's niche. The model's predicted drift capacity will be statistically compared to observed performance in these matched subsets using residual analysis and hypothesis testing.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first formal, niche-based assessment of a seismic capacity model's regional transferability, quantifying potential biases. It will deliver a generalizable validation framework and practical guidance, including potential adjustment factors, for applying regionally calibrated models globally, thereby improving seismic risk assessment in data-poor regions.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in seismology, directly importing and adapting the rigorous cross-regional validation framework from Species Distribution Modeling. It moves beyond qualitative 'similar region' comparisons to a quantitative, multi-parameter niche-matching process for validating empirical seismic models.

proposals.title: A Tiered, Regionally-Calibrated Framework for Global Seismic Liquefaction Capacity Prediction
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction evaluation models, often developed from region-specific databases, show significant performance variability when applied globally, leading to unreliable hazard assessments. This inconsistency stems from unaccounted-for regional differences in soil genesis, geologic history, and seismological characteristics. A unified, scalable framework is urgently needed to improve the reliability of liquefaction predictions for critical infrastructure and risk management worldwide.
proposals.hypothesis: A base liquefaction capacity model, calibrated on a high-quality global case history database, can be systematically adjusted using region-specific correction factors derived from the analysis of model residuals. These residuals are correlated with identifiable geotechnical, seismological, and geological parameters.
proposals.method: We will first expand and curate a global liquefaction case history database using platforms like DesignSafe, integrating Vs30, CPT, and SPT data with processed ground-motion records (using tools for high-pass corner-frequency selection). The Japanese capacity model (e.g., based on Vs) will serve as the initial base model. We will perform a regional residual analysis, partitioning data by tectonic and geologic province. Key parameters influencing residuals (e.g., soil age, depositional environment, attenuation characteristics) will be identified via regression and machine learning (e.g., random forests). The final framework will implement the base model with region-specific additive or multiplicative adjustment modules.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a novel, open-access predictive framework that moves beyond binary model validation to provide context-aware liquefaction assessment. It will deliver a publicly available, expanded global case history database and quantify the primary drivers of regional model bias, offering practitioners a scalable tool with defined uncertainty for different geologic settings.
proposals.novelty: The approach synthesizes global data synthesis and regional validation into a single, tiered framework, explicitly linking model residuals to measurable regional parameters rather than proposing entirely new models for each region. This creates a adaptable system that maintains a consistent physical basis while accounting for contextual geologic factors.

proposals.title: Regional Validation of a Seismic Capacity Correlation Model: Testing a Japanese-Derived Framework Against Global Case Histories
proposals.motivation: Seismic capacity correlation models, which link ground motion intensity to observed structural damage, are crucial for performance-based engineering and loss estimation. Many such models are region-specific, often derived from Japanese case histories due to their high-quality post-event data. The generalizability of these models to regions with differing construction practices, soil conditions, and seismicity is a critical, unresolved question in seismic risk analysis. Validating these models globally is essential for improving the reliability of seismic assessments outside Japan.
proposals.hypothesis: The seismic capacity correlation model derived from Japanese case histories will show systematic regional biases when applied to other tectonic settings, but these biases can be corrected by incorporating region-specific site and ground-motion parameters. The core relationships may remain valid if key explanatory variables are properly accounted for.
proposals.method: We will compile a global database of case histories (observed damage vs. ground motion) from pre-2026 events, leveraging platforms like DesignSafe and the NGA-West2 database. The Japanese model will be applied to predict damage for each case. We will statistically compare predictions to observations, stratified by region (e.g., California, New Zealand, Italy). The analysis will use mixed-effects regression to quantify regional random effects and test if biases are attenuated by including region-specific Vs30, depth-to-rock, and non-linear site response factors. High-pass filtering criteria from Brandenberg's prior work will ensure consistent ground-motion processing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative assessment of the portability of a key seismic fragility tool, directly informing international building code committees and risk modelers. It will produce either a validated global model or region-specific adjustment factors, enhancing the accuracy of seismic risk assessments worldwide. The curated global case-history database will also be a public resource for future research.
proposals.novelty: While regional comparisons exist for ground-motion models, a focused validation of a Japanese capacity correlation model against a globally diverse damage dataset is novel. The approach integrates site-specific geotechnical parameters (Vs30, depth) as explanatory variables for regional bias, moving beyond simple geographic scaling.

proposals.title: A Version-Controlled Framework for Probabilistic Geotechnical Site Response Analysis: Quantifying the Impact of Data and Model Lineage on Low-Probability Risk Estimates
proposals.motivation: Geotechnical reliability analyses for seismic hazards, such as site response in regions like the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta, are critically dependent on evolving subsurface models and ground-motion data. Current practices, while benefiting from databases like DesignSafe, lack a systematic method to track how incremental updates to input data, velocity profiles, or processing parameters propagate through complex probabilistic workflows like subset simulation. This obscures the epistemic contribution of data curation to final risk estimates, hindering reproducible and auditable science.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating formal data versioning principles with subset simulation will create an auditable lineage that quantitatively reveals how low-probability failure estimates are sensitive to specific revisions in geotechnical data and model assumptions, beyond the aleatory variability typically captured.
proposals.method: We will develop a Python framework that wraps a subset simulation engine (for efficient estimation of low probability of liquefaction or excessive settlement) with a Git-like version control system for geotechnical data artifacts. Key model components—shear-wave velocity profiles, attenuation relationships, and high-pass filter corner frequencies—will be stored as versioned objects in a Data Version Control (DVC) pipeline. Each subset simulation run will be linked to a precise commit hash of its inputs and intermediate subset states, enabling differential analysis. Sensitivity of the final failure probability to discrete changes in the lineage will be computed using a novel metric comparing versioned simulation branches.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new open-source tool for version-controlled probabilistic geotechnical analysis, enhancing the reproducibility standards exemplified by platforms like DesignSafe. It will provide a methodological advance for quantifying epistemic uncertainty stemming from data revision history, directly supporting more transparent and explainable seismic risk assessments.
proposals.novelty: While subset simulation and version control are established independently, their fusion to create a traceable lineage for low-probability geotechnical risk is novel. This moves beyond static database versioning to dynamically link probabilistic computational workflows with the evolution of their underlying data and models.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed Machine Learning Framework for Remote Liquefaction Detection Using a Global Digital Twin Prior
proposals.motivation: Rapid, accurate post-earthquake liquefaction identification is critical for emergency response and infrastructure assessment, but current methods rely on time-consuming and often dangerous field reconnaissance. Remote sensing offers a scalable alternative, but existing algorithms suffer from high false-positive rates and lack validation against physical ground truth, limiting their reliability for engineering decisions. This research directly addresses this validation flaw by constructing a physics-constrained digital prior of susceptible sediments to ground remote sensing interpretations.
proposals.hypothesis: A machine learning model, trained on a global historical dataset of remote sensing signals and legacy field reports and constrained by a pre-event digital twin of geotechnical susceptibility, can reliably detect liquefaction surface manifestations in new events without new field visits. The integration of physical priors will significantly reduce false positives compared to purely data-driven remote sensing methods.
proposals.method: We will first construct a global digital twin prior by fusing pre-2026 global geological databases (e.g., OneGeology) and geotechnical databases (e.g., USGS VS30) with high-resolution satellite imagery to map liquefaction-susceptible sediments. A physics-informed neural network (PINN) will be developed, integrating constitutive model constraints for pore pressure generation. This model will be trained on a curated global dataset from the DesignSafe platform, co-registering optical/InSAR remote sensing data from past earthquakes (e.g., 2011 Christchurch, 2018 Palu) with legacy field reconnaissance reports. For validation, the trained model will be applied to withheld events, and its performance will be quantitatively compared against traditional image classification models.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, open-source framework for rapid, remote liquefaction detection that does not require post-event ground truth, directly enhancing post-disaster situational awareness. It will contribute a novel, globally applicable 'digital ground truth' prior for geotechnical remote sensing and demonstrate the efficacy of physics-informed machine learning for solving inverse problems in geotechnical earthquake engineering.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of a globally scalable digital twin of geotechnical conditions as a constraining prior for remote sensing classification, moving beyond region-specific models. Furthermore, the application of a physics-informed machine learning architecture to this problem integrates geotechnical mechanics directly into the detection algorithm, a significant advance over purely spectral or texture-based image analysis.

proposals.title: A Remote Sensing Framework for Liquefaction Detection: Calibrating Spectral Fingerprints with Seismic and Geotechnical Instrumentation
proposals.motivation: Rapid and accurate identification of soil liquefaction after major earthquakes is critical for infrastructure assessment and emergency response, yet current methods rely heavily on time-consuming and often hazardous field reconnaissance. This creates a significant data gap, especially in remote or inaccessible regions. A remote sensing-based detection method would provide a transformative, scalable solution for post-event impact analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: Liquefaction manifests as a detectable multi-spectral and hyperspectral signature in pre- and post-event satellite imagery, characterized by combined changes in surface moisture, texture, and sediment composition. This spectral fingerprint can be uniquely correlated with ground-truth data from seismic ground motion and geotechnical instrumentation.
proposals.method: We will leverage the DesignSafe cyberinfrastructure to integrate a curated database of co-located events, including pre-2026 satellite imagery (e.g., Landsat, Sentinel-2, WorldView), seismic records (ground motion and attenuation parameters), and InSAR ground deformation from past earthquakes in susceptible regions like the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta. Using a machine learning architecture (e.g., a Random Forest or Convolutional Neural Network classifier), we will train models to identify spectral changes indicative of liquefaction. The models will be calibrated against instrumental metrics of shaking intensity (e.g., PGA, PGV) and geotechnical proxies for pore pressure generation derived from Vs30 and regional soil data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first validated, instrumentally-calibrated framework for remote liquefaction detection, moving beyond qualitative photo-interpretation. It will contribute a new, open-source analysis tool to the DesignSafe ecosystem and generate a novel database linking spectral signatures to geotechnical and seismic parameters for future research.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct conceptual analogy to exoplanet atmospheric detection, treating liquefaction as a 'state change' with a remote spectral signature, and in its rigorous fusion of remote sensing data with ground-motion processing and site-response parameters to create a physics-informed detection model.

proposals.title: Physics-Constrained Ensemble Modeling of Subsurface Uncertainty for Probabilistic Liquefaction Hazard Mapping in the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta
proposals.motivation: Liquefaction hazard maps are critical for risk mitigation in regions like California's Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta, but they are often based on simplified or deterministic subsurface models derived from sparse data. This leads to high epistemic uncertainty, which is rarely quantified, potentially misdirecting resources for site investigations and undermining confidence in hazard assessments. A framework that explicitly generates and propagates plausible subsurface realizations into a probabilistic hazard product is urgently needed for more rational risk management.
proposals.hypothesis: An ensemble of subsurface models, generated to honor statistical distributions of geophysical parameters (e.g., Vs30) and physical constraints of soil behavior, will produce a spatially explicit probabilistic liquefaction hazard map that more accurately quantifies both the likelihood of liquefaction and the associated predictive uncertainty than current deterministic or simplified probabilistic approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid framework using the DesignSafe cyberinfrastructure. First, we will use surface geology and regional Vs30 databases (e.g., from Brandenberg's prior work) to define prior statistical distributions for subsurface layers. A Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm, informed by physical constraints (e.g., soil mechanics relationships, wave attenuation principles), will generate an ensemble of 1D subsurface column realizations per map cell. Each realization will be evaluated using standard engineering procedures (e.g., Boulanger & Idriss, 2014) for a suite of ground motions from the NGA-West2 database. The output will be probabilistic hazard curves and maps of liquefaction triggering probability and severity, alongside a map of ensemble standard deviation to delineate uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, open-source workflow for physics-informed probabilistic liquefaction hazard mapping that explicitly quantifies subsurface uncertainty. It will deliver a practical tool for regional planners to identify zones where hazard is both high and uncertain, thereby optimizing the allocation of detailed site investigations. The ensemble modeling framework will be a transferable contribution to the broader field of performance-based geotechnical earthquake engineering.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using surface data not for direct prediction, but to generate a physics-constrained ensemble of subsurface models, explicitly treating geotechnical parameters as spatially variable random fields within a rigorous probabilistic hazard framework—a significant advance over deterministic factor-of-safety maps or simplified probabilistic methods that neglect cross-correlation and physical constraints.

proposals.title: A Data Assimilation Framework for Probabilistic Reconstruction of Time-Variant Subsurface Conditions and Liquefaction Susceptibility
proposals.motivation: Liquefaction hazard assessments are typically based on static, contemporary site characterization, ignoring decades of natural and anthropogenic changes that alter soil state. This is a critical limitation in regions like the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta, where subsidence, groundwater fluctuations, and land-use changes have dynamically modified geotechnical properties. A time-variant hazard model is needed to accurately assess risk for critical infrastructure with long lifespans and to understand the legacy of past events and interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: The time-varying subsurface conditions governing liquefaction susceptibility (e.g., relative density, fines content) can be probabilistically reconstructed by assimilating sparse, direct geotechnical snapshots with continuous, multi-decadal remote sensing and environmental proxy data.
proposals.method: We will develop an ensemble Kalman filter (EnKF) framework to assimilate heterogeneous, multi-temporal data. The state vector will include key soil parameters, evolved through simplified physics-based models of consolidation and sedimentation. Observational data will include historic Vs profiles (from the researcher's existing Delta database), InSAR-derived surface deformation time series (e.g., from Sentinel-1), well hydrographs, and classified satellite imagery for land-use history. The EnKF will generate an ensemble of plausible subsurface histories, quantifying epistemic uncertainty from data sparsity and model error.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first open, probabilistic framework for reconstructing the evolution of site-specific liquefaction susceptibility. It will directly quantify how epistemic uncertainty in past conditions propagates into present-day hazard estimates. The methodology will be demonstrated as a Jupyter notebook workflow on DesignSafe, enabling community application and extension.
proposals.novelty: While data assimilation is common in hydrology and weather, its application to reconstruct historical geotechnical site conditions is novel. The fusion of InSAR time series as a proxy for subsurface state evolution, within a formal probabilistic inverse framework, moves beyond traditional static or deterministic historical corrections.

proposals.title: A State-and-Transition Model for Time-Variant Liquefaction Susceptibility: Integrating Anthropogenic and Natural Drivers in the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction hazard maps treat site conditions as static, ignoring the dynamic effects of human activity and natural processes. In regions like the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta, decades of land reclamation, agricultural water use, and subsidence have fundamentally altered soil profiles and groundwater regimes, potentially changing liquefaction risk over time. A dynamic framework is needed to account for this operational history for more accurate, time-sensitive risk assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: A site's liquefaction resistance can be modeled as a dynamic state variable, where transitions between states of susceptibility are driven by quantifiable sequences of anthropogenic and natural disturbances, analogous to ecological succession.
proposals.method: We will develop a state-and-transition model (STM) framework where each state is defined by a vector of geotechnical parameters (e.g., Vs30, CPT profiles, groundwater depth). Transition rules will be formulated using pre-2026 geotechnical principles and calibrated against historical data from the Delta region. The model will be implemented as a probabilistic graph or Markov chain, with transition probabilities conditioned on disturbance 'events' (e.g., levee construction, drought-induced drawdown) cataloged in a spatiotemporal database. Model validation will use case histories of liquefaction performance and non-performance from past earthquakes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, time-variant framework for liquefaction susceptibility assessment that explicitly incorporates site history. It will deliver prototype dynamic hazard maps for the Delta region and a conceptual model transferable to other anthropogenically modified environments. The research will bridge geotechnical engineering and ecological modeling concepts.
proposals.novelty: The approach fundamentally inverts the static site assumption pervasive in practice by treating soil profiles as dynamically evolving systems. It is novel in its direct adaptation of ecological succession and state-and-transition models, well-established in other fields, to a core geotechnical hazard problem.

proposals.title: An Interactive Web-GIS Platform for Scenario-Based Liquefaction Susceptibility Modeling in Data-Scarce Regions
proposals.motivation: Regional liquefaction hazard assessment is critical for risk mitigation but often hindered by a lack of detailed subsurface data, particularly in large, geologically complex regions like the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta. Existing susceptibility maps are static and obscure model assumptions, limiting their utility for planners and engineers who need to explore scenarios and understand uncertainties. A transparent, interactive tool that leverages commonly available surface geology and geophysical proxies can democratize access to advanced hazard modeling.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating simplified, physics-informed liquefaction models (e.g., based on Vs30 and geologic age) into an interactive Web-GIS platform will enable accurate regional susceptibility screening and foster greater trust and understanding among stakeholders by making model parameters and uncertainties directly explorable.
proposals.method: The platform will be built using a client-server architecture with a Python/Flask or Django backend and a JavaScript frontend (e.g., Leaflet/MapLibre). It will integrate a regional geospatial database of surface geology, Vs30 estimates (from existing maps or topographic slope proxies), and groundwater data. The core model will implement a simplified stress-based procedure, such as the Boulanger & Idriss (2014) framework, using Vs30 as a proxy for cyclic resistance. The interactive interface will allow users to adjust input parameters (e.g., PGA from selected ground motion models), run scenario earthquakes, and visualize resulting factor of safety maps with uncertainty layers derived from parameter sensitivity analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-access, operational tool for rapid regional liquefaction screening, directly supporting the planning and reconnaissance phases of engineering projects. It will advance the field by demonstrating how interactive visualization can be used not just for display, but for model exploration and validation, thereby improving communication of geotechnical hazard uncertainties. The platform will be designed for integration with cyberinfrastructure like DesignSafe, promoting open science and reproducibility in natural hazards research.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of a simplified, yet physically grounded, liquefaction model with an interactive Web-GIS interface specifically designed for scenario exploration and sensitivity analysis, moving beyond static map products. This transforms the model from a black-box into a transparent tool for hypothesis testing and stakeholder engagement in data-scarce contexts.

proposals.title: Time-Dependent Liquefaction Fragility for Critical Infrastructure: Integrating Non-Stationary Ground Motion and Soil Capacity Models
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction fragility models are largely static, using time-invariant soil and hydrological parameters, which fails to capture the evolving risk to infrastructure from climate-driven changes like rising groundwater and from soil property changes over an asset's lifecycle. This static approach is inadequate for long-term risk management and targeted mitigation planning for critical assets in regions like the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta, where subsidence and hydrological shifts are pronounced. A dynamic, asset-specific framework is needed to forecast future vulnerability under non-stationary environmental conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: The probability of liquefaction-induced failure for a specific infrastructure asset can be modeled as a dynamic function of time, where both the seismic demand (via ground-motion models) and geotechnical capacity (via cyclic stress ratio) are treated as non-stationary processes influenced by measurable environmental trends.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian hierarchical model that fuses time-series data from geotechnical databases (e.g., CPT logs, Vs profiles) and hydrological monitoring (groundwater levels, precipitation) to project future soil capacity. This will be integrated with region-specific ground-motion models and attenuation relationships to generate non-stationary seismic demand. For each target asset (e.g., a bridge pier), we will compute a time-evolving fragility function using a Monte Carlo framework that propagates uncertainties in both demand and capacity projections, implemented within the DesignSafe cyberinfrastructure for open, reproducible science.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first operational framework for generating dynamic, asset-specific liquefaction fragility curves that account for non-stationary environmental forcings. It will directly enable lifecycle probabilistic risk assessments for infrastructure owners and provide a methodology for prioritizing mitigation investments based on forecasted vulnerability, rather than historical snapshots.
proposals.novelty: The synthesis of non-stationary seismic hazard analysis with time-variant geotechnical capacity models for asset-specific fragility represents a significant advance beyond current practice, which treats these elements as decoupled and stationary. The explicit modeling of how climate-influenced hydrology dynamically alters fragility for a single structure is a novel contribution to performance-based earthquake engineering.

proposals.title: Inverting Spatial Liquefaction Evidence to Constrain Source Parameters of Pre-Instrumental Earthquakes
proposals.motivation: A significant challenge in seismic hazard analysis is the incomplete historical record of large earthquakes, particularly in regions with long recurrence intervals. Paleoliquefaction studies provide critical evidence of past events, but current methods primarily focus on identifying their occurrence and approximate location. There is a need to quantitatively extract more detailed source characteristics, such as magnitude and fault geometry, from the spatial pattern of liquefaction evidence to better constrain seismic source models and improve probabilistic seismic hazard assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial distribution and intensity of paleoliquefaction features (e.g., sand blows, dikes) are systematically related to the magnitude, source geometry, and rupture directivity of the causative earthquake. This relationship can be inverted using ground motion prediction equations (GMPEs) and site-specific geotechnical models to estimate key source parameters.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian inversion framework. The forward model will combine a ground-motion model (e.g., a Next Generation Attenuation, NGA, relation) with a regionally-calibrated geotechnical model (linking peak ground acceleration to liquefaction triggering) and a spatial interpolation of site-condition proxies (e.g., Vs30 from existing databases like that of the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta). The inversion algorithm will use a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampler to explore the parameter space (magnitude, location, fault strike/dip) that best explains the observed spatial pattern of liquefaction evidence. The tool will be tested on well-documented historical events (e.g., 1811-1812 New Madrid sequence) before application to prehistoric cases.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, open-source computational tool for the quantitative interpretation of paleoliquefaction data, hosted on platforms like DesignSafe to ensure reproducibility. It will advance the field of paleoseismology from qualitative recognition to quantitative source characterization. The resulting constraints on prehistoric earthquake parameters will directly feed into fault source models for seismic hazard maps.
proposals.novelty: While forward modeling of liquefaction for known earthquakes is common, a formal probabilistic inversion to solve for earthquake source characteristics from liquefaction patterns is a significant and undeveloped inverse problem. This approach uniquely integrates GMPEs, geotechnical models, and Bayesian inference in a way not previously applied to paleoseismic data.

proposals.title: Developing a Systematic Database of Non-Liquefaction Case Histories to Constrain and Improve Susceptibility Models
proposals.motivation: Current liquefaction susceptibility models are trained and validated on a biased dataset dominated by case histories where liquefaction was observed. This creates a critical knowledge gap regarding the conditions that prevent liquefaction, leading to potentially over-conservative and poorly constrained models. This bias impedes accurate site-specific hazard assessment and efficient geotechnical design, particularly in regions like the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta with complex soil profiles.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematically compiling and analyzing a database of well-documented non-liquefaction cases will reveal critical geotechnical, geological, and seismic parameters that define the boundary between liquefiable and non-liquefiable soil response, leading to more accurate and physically constrained susceptibility models.
proposals.method: We will leverage the DesignSafe-CI cyberinfrastructure to create a dual, relational database schema for both liquefaction and non-liquefaction cases. The framework will extend existing ground-motion processing and Vs30 databases (e.g., from NGA-West2) by mandating standardized fields for CPT, SPT, Vs, and groundwater data, paired with rigorously processed ground motions (using tools like the high-pass corner-frequency review tool) for events above a PGA/PGV threshold. A key technical step will be developing and applying a transparent, rule-based algorithm to classify cases from post-earthquake reconnaissance reports, dense instrument arrays (e.g., CSMIP), and the literature, flagging sites with sufficient shaking but no surface manifestation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first publicly available, curated registry of non-liquefaction case histories, directly providing the counterfactual data needed for robust model testing and development. It will reduce redundant site investigations by clarifying where liquefaction triggering models are applicable and will foster a more complete understanding of seismic soil performance through open data sharing on the DesignSafe platform.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in geotechnical earthquake engineering for its systematic adaptation of the 'negative data' registry concept from clinical science to address publication bias. While databases of liquefaction cases exist, a concerted effort to build a co-equal database of non-occurrences using standardized, shareable cyberinfrastructure is unprecedented.

proposals.title: Bridging the Scale Gap in Geotechnics: A Downscaling Framework for Calibrating Laboratory Element Tests with Field Performance Databases
proposals.motivation: A persistent challenge in geotechnical engineering is the scale gap between controlled laboratory element tests and complex, full-scale field performance. This gap introduces significant uncertainty in model validation and site-specific hazard assessment, particularly for phenomena like liquefaction triggering and post-cyclic deformation. While extensive field case history databases exist (e.g., from seismic events), methods to systematically use this data to inform and validate lab testing protocols are lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: Statistical downscaling techniques, analogous to those used in climate science, can be applied to geotechnical field databases to identify the critical material states and stress paths governing field-scale failure. This will enable the design of laboratory tests that are not just geometrically scaled but are physically calibrated simulations of field behavior.
proposals.method: We will leverage the DesignSafe cyberinfrastructure and curated field databases (e.g., liquefaction case histories, CPT logs, Vs profiles) as the 'historical climate' dataset. Using a machine learning framework, specifically a supervised learning model (e.g., a Gradient Boosting Regressor or a simple Neural Network), we will map high-level field conditions (e.g., regional geology, event magnitude, depth) to the inferred critical state parameters and stress paths required to replicate observed field performance. This mapping will be used to define boundary conditions and initial states for numerical simulations of lab element tests (e.g., cyclic simple shear), creating a closed calibration loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, data-driven framework for designing geotechnical laboratory testing programs that are fundamentally validated against field observations. It will increase confidence in predictive models by providing a systematic method to ground truth lab data. The outcome is a more reliable translation of element test results to field-scale performance predictions for seismic hazard applications.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct conceptual borrowing of climate model downscaling methodology to solve a core geotechnical scaling problem. It moves beyond traditional empirical correlation by using machine learning to extract the underlying physical states connecting lab and field scales, a method not yet applied in this domain.

proposals.title: Empirical Validation of Non-Gaussian Correlation Models for Soil Response via Database-Informed Cyclic Simple Shear Testing
proposals.motivation: Advanced reliability methods for geotechnical systems, such as level-crossing analyses, increasingly employ non-Gaussian models to capture realistic extreme-value statistics and system correlation. However, the selection of appropriate correlation functions (e.g., based on translation processes, copulas, or spectral methods) remains largely theoretical, lacking direct validation against physical failure data. This gap limits confidence in reliability predictions for critical infrastructure, particularly in regions like the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta with complex, spatially variable soils.
proposals.hypothesis: High-fidelity laboratory tests, designed to replicate field-recorded loading sequences, will reveal systematic biases in existing non-Gaussian correlation models, enabling the empirical identification and calibration of the most physically accurate functions for predicting soil failure.
proposals.method: We will extract realistic, multi-directional stress-time histories from the NGA-West2 and DesignSafe databases for specific Delta region sites. These sequences will drive a program of cyclic simple shear tests on reconstituted Delta peat and clay specimens. The experimental outputs—cycles to liquefaction or strain accumulation—will be treated as observed 'failure' data. We will then compare these results to predictions from competing non-Gaussian models (e.g., using the translation process with different correlation structures, fitted to the input motion statistics) via a Bayesian updating framework. The model yielding the highest likelihood for the observed laboratory failures will be identified as optimal.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first physically validated framework for selecting non-Gaussian correlation functions in geotechnical reliability analysis. It will deliver a curated dataset of database-informed laboratory tests and provide practical guidance for reliability practitioners on model selection, moving the field from mathematical convenience to empirical justification.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, physical benchmarking of abstract correlation functions using controlled experiments, a fusion of high-fidelity laboratory geotechnics and advanced reliability theory that has not been attempted. This moves validation beyond synthetic numerical comparisons to evidence-based model selection.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Necessity of Pre-Event Baseline Data for Accurate Co-Seismic Site Response and Ground Deformation Analysis
proposals.motivation: Current seismic hazard and site response analyses often rely on post-event data, assuming it sufficiently characterizes site conditions. However, this may introduce significant error in quantifying co-seismic changes, such as shear wave velocity reduction or permanent ground deformation, which are critical for understanding soil nonlinearity and damage patterns. The assumption of post-event data sufficiency remains largely untested, potentially biasing ground-motion models and engineering assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: Pre-event baseline geotechnical and geophysical data are a critical prerequisite for accurately measuring the magnitude of co-seismic site changes; analyses using only post-event data will systematically misestimate these changes, leading to errors in inferred soil nonlinearity and ground deformation.
proposals.method: We will leverage the DesignSafe cyberinfrastructure and existing regional databases (e.g., CESMD, NSMP) to compile paired pre- and post-event datasets for sites that experienced strong shaking from well-recorded earthquakes (e.g., 2014 South Napa, 2019 Ridgecrest sequences). Using the researcher's established high-pass filtering and processing tools, we will compute site amplification functions and shear-wave velocity profiles from both datasets. We will then invert for co-seismic changes using the full pre/post pair and compare results to inversions that artificially omit the pre-event data, using only a post-event baseline assumption. Statistical analysis will quantify the error distributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, data-driven test of a common assumption in earthquake engineering, establishing quantitative guidelines for when pre-event data is essential. It will directly improve protocols for post-earthquake reconnaissance and site response analysis by highlighting the risk of biased conclusions drawn from post-event data alone.
proposals.novelty: While the value of dense instrumentation is recognized, the explicit inversion of the 'post-event sufficiency' assumption—treating it as a testable hypothesis rather than an operational given—is a novel and critical reframing for the field. It connects database resources directly to a fundamental methodological question in measurement.

proposals.title: A Systematic Database of Surface Rupture Zone Characteristics for Major Earthquakes: Integrating High-Resolution Mapping, Site Response, and Ground-Motion Attenuation
proposals.motivation: Current ground-motion prediction and site response models rely heavily on point-source or simplified fault representations, lacking comprehensive constraints from the full 3D complexity of surface rupture zones. This gap limits our ability to predict localized strong ground motions and permanent ground deformation, which are critical for infrastructure resilience. The 2019 Ridgecrest earthquake sequence underscored the prevalence of distributed, multi-fault ruptures not captured by existing databases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematically mapped surface rupture characteristics—including primary trace geometry, distributed fracturing, and associated near-surface shear-wave velocity (Vs) variations—exert a first-order control on the spatial pattern of high-frequency ground-motion attenuation and site amplification within the near-field region.
proposals.method: We will expand the methods demonstrated in the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta study to entire rupture zones. For selected historic and recent events (e.g., 1992 Landers, 2016 Kaikōura), we will systematically digitize the full surface rupture and secondary features from high-resolution lidar, optical imagery, and field surveys. These geometric datasets will be integrated into the DesignSafe cyberinfrastructure. We will then apply a region-specific Vs30 mapping and ground-motion processing workflow, using the high-pass corner-frequency review tool to consistently process strong-motion records. Attenuation relationships will be analyzed as a function of distance-from-rupture-plane, measured perpendicular to the locally mapped trace.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first open, standardized database linking detailed rupture zone geometry to observed ground motions and Vs profiles. It will directly improve empirical ground-motion models by providing a physically-based, spatially-variable distance metric. The database will serve as a benchmark for validating physics-based rupture simulations and inform the placement of critical infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have mapped individual ruptures, none have systematically integrated the full 3D rupture zone geometry with region-specific site characterization and processed ground-motion data into a unified, open-access resource designed for attenuation analysis. The novel distance metric moves beyond simplistic fault-distance measures.

proposals.title: Inverting Building Damage Patterns to Constrain Ground Shaking Intensity: A Regional Case Study for the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta
proposals.motivation: Instrumental ground-motion records are sparse, especially in regions of moderate seismicity, creating significant uncertainty in seismic hazard assessment and ground-motion models. The Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta region, with its complex, soft soils and developing infrastructure, is a prime example where shaking estimates from sparse instruments may not capture local site effects. A method to infer shaking from pervasive post-earthquake damage observations would fill critical data gaps and improve loss estimation.
proposals.hypothesis: Building damage patterns aggregated at a regional scale, when combined with pre-event site-condition databases and structural fragility models, can be inverted to produce a spatially continuous map of ground shaking intensity (e.g., PGA, Sa) that is consistent with sparse instrumental records and explains observed damage distributions.
proposals.method: We will leverage the DesignSafe cyberinfrastructure to access and manage regional building inventories, shear-wave velocity (Vs30) databases, and damage surveys. The core inverse problem will be framed in a Bayesian framework, treating the shaking field as unknown parameters and the damage data as observations. We will use a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm to sample the posterior distribution of shaking intensity, conditioned on prior constraints from ground-motion models (e.g., NGA-West2) and likelihood functions derived from HAZUS-compatible fragility curves. The approach will explicitly incorporate spatial correlation of shaking and uncertainty in building vulnerability classification.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, data-driven methodology for generating 'pseudo-intensity measure' maps in instrument-sparse regions, directly informing seismic hazard validation and site-response studies.
