It will demonstrate a practical fusion of geotechnical site data, structural engineering fragility, and statistical inference to solve an inverse problem in earthquake engineering, with results directly applicable to the Delta and similar regions.
proposals.novelty: While forward prediction of damage from shaking is standard, the explicit inversion of regional damage patterns to solve for the input shaking field is a non-trivial reversal of the typical workflow. The novelty lies in the integrated Bayesian framework that jointly resolves shaking intensity while rigorously propagating uncertainties from site conditions, fragility models, and data sparsity.

proposals.title: Quantifying Seismic Wall-Soil Separation and Gap Formation: A Physics-Based Framework for Improved Site Response and Retaining Wall Analysis
proposals.motivation: Current site response analyses and retaining wall design often assume perfect wall-soil contact, which may be violated during strong shaking, leading to nonlinear gap formation. This separation can significantly alter dynamic earth pressures, modify the seismic demand on structures, and is a potential but poorly quantified mechanism for permanent displacement. Addressing this gap is critical for accurate performance-based seismic design in regions with soft soils, such as the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that wall-soil separation during seismic shaking can be reliably modeled using a cohesive zone contact formulation, and that the resulting gap formation will significantly amplify high-frequency ground motions near the wall while reducing low-frequency inertial demands on the wall itself.
proposals.method: We will develop a 2D finite element model in OpenSees or a similar platform, incorporating a cohesive zone model with tension cutoff and frictional contact to simulate separation and re-contact. The soil domain will use a pressure-dependent multi-yield surface constitutive model, calibrated against regional Vs data from the DesignSafe database. The framework will be validated using centrifuge test data (pre-2026) and applied to a suite of recorded ground motions, processed with a standardized high-pass filter tool to ensure consistent input.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated numerical framework for simulating wall-soil separation, directly informing revisions to simplified seismic earth pressure models. It will provide new insights into how gap formation modifies free-field site response, contributing to more accurate site-specific ground motion predictions for critical infrastructure adjacent to earth-retaining structures.
proposals.novelty: While soil-structure interaction is well-studied, explicitly modeling the dynamic loss of contact for embedded walls using a cohesive zone approach within a comprehensive site response framework is novel. This integrates Brandenberg's expertise in site characterization, motion processing, and database-enabled open science to address a previously idealized boundary condition.

proposals.title: A Frequency-Dependent Equivalent Linear Framework for Site Response Analysis: Implementation and Validation in the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta
proposals.motivation: Current equivalent linear site response analyses, widely used in engineering practice, rely on strain-dependent but frequency-independent shear modulus and damping models. This simplification is known to be inaccurate, as laboratory data and field observations demonstrate significant modulus reduction and damping variation with excitation frequency, particularly in soft, saturated soils like those in the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta. Ignoring this frequency dependence can lead to mischaracterization of site amplification, especially for long-period structures and motions rich in low-frequency content, undermining the reliability of seismic hazard assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a frequency-dependent complex modulus model within an equivalent linear framework will significantly improve the accuracy of predicted site response spectra for soft soil sites, particularly at periods longer than 0.5 seconds, compared to standard frequency-independent methods.
proposals.method: We will implement the frequency-dependent model by Meng & Rix (2003) or a similar viscoelastic formulation into the open-source site response code Strata. The core algorithm will iteratively solve for effective strain while updating complex shear modulus G*(f, γ) and damping ξ(f, γ) at each frequency. Validation will use the extensive Vs database for the Delta region and select recorded ground motions from events like the 2014 South Napa earthquake. We will leverage the DesignSafe-CI cyberinfrastructure for high-throughput processing, systematic comparison against standard methods, and dissemination of the tool and processed results.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, open-source computational tool for frequency-dependent site response analysis, directly applicable to regional hazard studies. It will provide a quantitative assessment of the impact of frequency-dependent soil properties on site amplification in critical soft-soil regions, offering practitioners a path to more accurate ground motion predictions.
proposals.novelty: While frequency-dependent soil behavior is well-known in geomechanics, its integration into a practical, regionally validated equivalent linear analysis tool for engineering application is novel. This bridges a gap between high-fidelity laboratory models and the simplified methods used in practice for code-based and regional hazard analysis.

proposals.title: A Spectral Element Framework for High-Fidelity Site Response Analysis of Complex Layered Soils in the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of site-specific ground motions is critical for seismic hazard assessment, yet current 1D equivalent-linear and nonlinear methods struggle with complex, low-velocity layered profiles like those in the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta. These methods often fail to capture 3D wavefield effects, including basin-generated surface waves and complex interference patterns, which can significantly amplify shaking. A more physically rigorous, yet computationally efficient, numerical approach is needed to improve ground-motion predictions for critical infrastructure in these vulnerable regions.
proposals.hypothesis: The spectral element method (SEM), with its high-order accuracy and geometric flexibility, can efficiently model the full wavefield in complex, shallow soil layering, capturing frequency-dependent amplification and basin effects that are missed by conventional 1D site response analyses. We hypothesize that SEM-based simulations will reconcile discrepancies between observed ground motions and current 1D predictions for Delta sites.
proposals.method: We will develop a 3D spectral element model using the open-source code SPECFEM3D, tailored for near-surface applications. The model domain will incorporate high-resolution shear-wave velocity (Vs) profiles from the researcher's existing Delta database. We will implement a mesh coarsening strategy with a fine, high-order element discretization in the shallow, low-velocity layers to capture high-frequency content efficiently. Simulations will be driven by recorded rock motions, processed using the researcher's high-pass corner-frequency tool, and validated against ground-motion recordings from regional seismic events.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, open-source SEM workflow for site response, hosted on DesignSafe, making high-fidelity 3D wave propagation accessible to the engineering seismology community. It will deliver new insights into the frequency-dependent amplification behavior of Delta soils, directly informing region-specific ground-motion models and attenuation relationships. The results will provide a benchmark for evaluating and improving simplified 1D site response methodologies.
proposals.novelty: While SEM is established in global and regional seismology, its application is novel for engineering-scale site response analysis of shallow, complex soil columns. The integration of a region-specific Vs database with a high-performance computational wave propagation tool to systematically address site effects in a problematic geologic setting represents a significant advance beyond current practice.

proposals.title: Inversion of Retaining Wall Seismic Response for Site-Specific Shear Wave Velocity Profiles in the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta
proposals.motivation: Site-specific seismic response is critical for infrastructure resilience, yet soil profiles are often poorly characterized due to sparse and costly boreholes. The Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta region, with its complex, soft, and spatially variable soils, presents a significant site-response challenge. Instrumented retaining walls, which are increasingly common in infrastructure monitoring, provide a unique, underutilized source of dense ground motion data that could constrain subsurface models.
proposals.hypothesis: The recorded translational and rotational accelerations of a deeply embedded retaining wall during earthquake shaking contain sufficient information to invert for a one-dimensional, depth-varying shear wave velocity (Vs) profile of the adjacent soil column. The inversion will be most robust for profiles with strong impedance contrasts that generate identifiable resonant frequencies in the wall response.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian inversion framework using a database of recorded wall motions from DesignSafe and regional networks. The forward model will be a coupled soil-wall system simulated using finite-element methods (e.g., OpenSees). The inverse problem will be solved using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling to estimate the posterior distribution of Vs profiles, constrained by prior information from regional Vs databases (e.g., from the researcher's prior work). Key to the method is the careful processing of input motions using a standardized high-pass filter selection tool to ensure reliable input for the inversion.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, data-driven methodology for inferring soil properties at instrumented sites, reducing reliance on invasive testing. It will deliver a publicly available inversion tool on DesignSafe and provide new, high-resolution Vs estimates for critical regions like the Delta, directly improving ground motion prediction and seismic design practices.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies use surface arrays to infer site response, this approach uniquely treats the instrumented retaining wall itself as a large-scale, in-situ penetrometer. The inversion explicitly couples soil and structural dynamics, moving beyond simple transfer function analysis to directly solve for material properties.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Sufficiency of Microtremor HVSR for Predicting Nonlinear Site Response: A Validation Study Using Strong-Motion Records from the NGA-West2 Database
proposals.motivation: The Horizontal-to-Vertical Spectral Ratio (HVSR) from ambient noise (microtremors) is a widely used, low-cost technique for estimating a site's fundamental frequency and, by proxy, its Vs30. However, its reliability for predicting site amplification during strong, nonlinear shaking is debated. Given the reliance on microtremor HVSR for regional site characterization in seismic hazard models, a rigorous validation against recorded strong-motion data is critical for improving ground-motion prediction and engineering design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that site amplification functions derived solely from microtremor HVSR will show significant systematic deviations from empirically observed amplification during moderate-to-strong ground shaking, particularly at high frequencies and large strains, due to the method's inability to capture nonlinear soil behavior and higher-mode effects.
proposals.method: We will utilize the NGA-West2 and DesignSafe databases to compile a paired dataset of (1) published microtremor HVSR curves and (2) recorded strong-motion events (M>5, PGA>0.1g) at co-located stations. For each site-event pair, we will compute empirical site amplification from processed recordings using a standardized high-pass filtering tool (e.g., based on Brandenberg's prior work) and a regional reference rock condition. These empirical amplifications will be statistically compared to 1D linear-equivalent and nonlinear site response predictions generated using velocity profiles inverted from the microtremor HVSR curves. The analysis will focus on residuals as a function of shaking intensity and frequency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, data-driven framework for assessing the limitations of microtremor-only site characterization. It will deliver practical guidance on the shaking intensity thresholds and site conditions beyond which HVSR-based models become unreliable, directly informing site response practice and seismic code provisions.
proposals.novelty: While HVSR is commonly validated for frequency estimation, this study directly tests its sufficiency for predicting full amplification functions during real, strong earthquakes by leveraging a large, curated database of paired microtremor and strong-motion observations, a comparison not systematically undertaken pre-2026.

proposals.title: Beyond the Median Horizontal: A Three-Component Framework for Site Response Characterization in the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta
proposals.motivation: Current site response analysis and ground motion models predominantly rely on the median horizontal component, discarding valuable information contained in the vertical and individual horizontal components. This simplification may obscure anisotropic site effects, directional amplification, and the complex wavefield interactions prevalent in soft-soil, low-velocity regions like the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta. A more comprehensive characterization is needed to improve ground motion prediction and seismic hazard assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: Site amplification functions derived from the full three-component ground motion data will reveal significant directional and component-dependent site effects not captured by the median horizontal component, leading to more accurate and physically complete site response models.
proposals.method: We will utilize the extensive ground motion database from the DesignSafe cyberinfrastructure, focusing on events recorded in the Delta region. For selected stations, we will compute frequency-dependent horizontal-to-vertical spectral ratios (HVSR) for each individual horizontal component (e.g., N-S, E-W) and compare them to the standard median HVSR. We will also compute and compare site amplification functions relative to a reference rock condition for all three components using established processing tools, including careful high-pass corner-frequency selection. Statistical analysis will quantify the variability and systematic differences between components across different site conditions and event back-azimuths.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, component-explicit framework for site characterization, directly quantifying the information loss from using only the median horizontal. It will deliver practical guidance on when three-component analysis is critical for engineering applications and contribute improved, directionally-aware site terms for ground motion models in complex soil basins.
proposals.novelty: While three-component data is routinely collected, its systematic use for site characterization beyond the standard HVSR or median horizontal amplification is limited. This proposal explicitly inverts the reliance on the median by treating each component as an independent but related data source to uncover anisotropic site behavior.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Ground-Motion Model Incorporating HVSR Peak Frequency and Amplitude as Direct Predictors for Site Response in Active Tectonic Regions
proposals.motivation: Current ground-motion models (GMMs) rely heavily on time-averaged shear-wave velocity in the upper 30 meters (VS30) as the primary site parameter, which is expensive and logistically challenging to measure at scale. The Horizontal-to-Vertical Spectral Ratio (HVSR) technique provides a rapid, low-cost proxy for site resonance but is not yet formally integrated into GMMs as a predictor variable. This gap limits the ability to refine site-specific hazard assessments, particularly in regions like California's sedimentary basins and deltas where complex subsurface conditions exist.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating HVSR-derived parameters—specifically the peak frequency (f0) and amplitude (A0)—as continuous predictor variables in a ground-motion model will significantly improve the prediction of site amplification and reduce aleatory variability compared to models using VS30 alone.
proposals.method: We will compile a large, regionally consistent database of ground motions and paired HVSR measurements from the DesignSafe cyberinfrastructure and other public repositories. Using a mixed-effects regression framework, we will develop a new GMM where the site term is a function of f0, A0, and potentially their interaction, alongside source and path terms. The model will be calibrated using processed ground motions, applying high-pass corner-frequency selection tools to ensure consistency. We will test the model's performance against traditional VS30-based models using statistical tests for residual analysis and variance reduction.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first fully operational GMM that directly uses HVSR data, providing a practical tool for site-specific seismic hazard analysis where VS30 is unknown. It will quantify the reduction in aleatory uncertainty achievable with HVSR constraints, advancing the goal of precision in ground-motion prediction. The resulting model and database will be shared openly via DesignSafe to enable community validation and application.
proposals.novelty: While HVSR is widely used for site classification, its direct incorporation as a continuous predictor in a physics-informed empirical GMM is novel. This moves beyond using HVSR for categorical binning, instead leveraging its full informational content to dynamically modulate the site response function within a standard GMM framework.

proposals.title: A Standardized Parameterization of HVSR Peaks for Site Characterization and Ground-Motion Modeling
proposals.motivation: The Horizontal-to-Vertical Spectral Ratio (HVSR) method is a widely used, low-cost tool for estimating site fundamental frequency (f0) and inferring subsurface structure. However, its application in quantitative site-response and ground-motion models is hindered by a lack of standardized parameters describing peak shape, amplitude, and broadening. This inconsistency limits the integration of HVSR data into regional databases and physics-based models, particularly in complex sedimentary basins like the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a standardized set of parameters, derived from a consistent peak-picking and curve-fitting algorithm, can robustly characterize HVSR peaks and correlate with key geotechnical properties (e.g., Vs30, depth to impedance contrast) and site-response behavior.
proposals.method: We will develop an automated Python tool that processes HVSR curves to extract a standardized parameter set: fundamental frequency (f0), peak amplitude (A0), peak width at half amplitude (HWHM), and a shape parameter (e.g., from a Lorentzian or Gaussian fit). The algorithm will build on existing processing standards (e.g., SESAME guidelines) and integrate with the high-pass corner-frequency review tool from Brandenberg's prior work to ensure data quality. We will apply this tool to a large, region-specific database of ambient noise measurements (e.g., from the Delta region) archived on DesignSafe, statistically analyzing the derived parameters against independent Vs profiles and recorded earthquake ground motions to establish empirical relationships.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with an open-source tool and a formalized parameter set for HVSR quantification, enabling consistent metadata generation for site databases. It will advance the use of HVSR beyond f0 estimation, offering new metrics for site classification and improving the constraint of 1D site-response models used in ground-motion prediction and attenuation studies.
proposals.novelty: While HVSR is common, the systematic derivation of a multi-parameter, standardized descriptor set for peak morphology, integrated with open-science cyberinfrastructure (DesignSafe) and region-specific databases, represents a novel step towards making HVSR a more quantitative and interoperable tool for engineering seismology.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Spatiotemporal Evolution of Seismic Site Response in Deltaic Peatlands Using Dense Arrays and InSAR
proposals.motivation: In the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta and similar subsiding peatlands, ongoing peat oxidation and thinning fundamentally alter near-surface geotechnical properties, including shear wave velocity (Vs). This dynamic ground condition challenges the assumption of static site amplification factors in seismic hazard models. Pre-2026, the temporal dimension of this site effect evolution is poorly constrained, posing a risk to the accuracy of long-term seismic risk assessments for critical infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that peat thinning and subsidence produce measurable, spatially variable changes in Vs30 and site amplification over decadal timescales, and that these changes can be predicted by integrating geodetic measurements of subsidence with geophysical site characterization.
proposals.method: We will deploy a dense network of nodal seismometers across a subsidence gradient in the Delta to collect new ambient noise data for HVSR and MASW analysis, directly measuring current Vs profiles. These will be integrated into a spatiotemporal database on DesignSafe, alongside historical Vs measurements from past studies (e.g., Brandenberg et al., 2022). We will correlate changes in Vs30 with time-series InSAR (Sentinel-1) subsidence maps and peat thickness data. A region-specific ground-motion model (GMM) scaling framework will be developed to attenuate the influence of static site terms and isolate the temporal component of site response.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantified model of time-dependent site amplification in organic soils, directly informing updates to building codes and seismic design practices in subsiding regions. It will deliver an open-access spatiotemporal Vs database and a methodological framework for monitoring dynamic site effects, extending the utility of platforms like DesignSafe for time-variant hazard analysis.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has characterized spatial variability of Delta soils, this proposal uniquely focuses on resolving the *temporal* change in seismic site response by fusing repeat geophysical surveys with continuous geodetic monitoring, moving beyond a static 'snapshot' model of site conditions.

proposals.title: A Multi-Hazard Risk Framework for the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta: Integrating Seismic Site Response, Subsidence, and Hydrology for Regional Infrastructure Assessment
proposals.motivation: The Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta is a critical and vulnerable region, facing compounding hazards from seismic shaking, land subsidence, and fluctuating hydrology. Pre-2026 risk assessments often treat these hazards in isolation, failing to capture their complex interactions, which can amplify damage to levees, water conveyance infrastructure, and communities. A unified, physics-based regional framework is urgently needed to inform resilient infrastructure investment and emergency planning.
proposals.hypothesis: The integrated, time-dependent effects of groundwater-driven subsidence and seasonal hydrology significantly alter near-surface shear-wave velocity (Vs) profiles and ground-motion amplification, leading to regional seismic risk estimates that differ substantially from those derived from static geotechnical models.
proposals.method: The framework will be built on the DesignSafe-CI cyberinfrastructure, leveraging its database and high-performance computing capabilities. We will first develop a regional 3D geotechnical model by integrating existing Vs databases (e.g., from the researcher's prior Delta work) with InSAR-derived subsidence time series and hydrological models. A modular workflow will then couple this dynamic earth model with region-specific ground-motion models and geotechnical amplification factors, using OpenSHA for probabilistic seismic hazard analysis. Risk to levee networks will be assessed via fragility curves within a geographic information system (GIS) platform, enabling scenario-based loss estimation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first open, reproducible regional risk assessment framework for the Delta that explicitly couples seismic, subsidence, and hydrological processes. It will deliver new maps of time-variant site amplification and associated levee fragility, providing a critical tool for state and federal agencies like the Delta Stewardship Council and USACE. The modular architecture will serve as a template for multi-hazard assessment in other subsiding, seismically active coastal regions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the dynamic coupling of a subsidence-hydrology model with a regional seismic site response framework to create a time-dependent earth model for hazard analysis, moving beyond the standard assumption of static subsurface conditions. This integration of the researcher's expertise in site effects, motion processing, and database management into a multi-physics risk workflow is unprecedented for the Delta system.

proposals.title: Quantifying Lateral Spreading Displacement Demands on Masonry and Wood Frame Structures in Trona and Argus, California
proposals.motivation: Lateral spreading during earthquakes causes significant damage to structures founded on liquefiable soils, yet current building codes and fragility models often lack site-specific displacement demands for common building typologies. The communities of Trona and Argus in California, situated on susceptible alluvial deposits and with a high concentration of vulnerable masonry and wood frame buildings, represent an ideal case study to bridge this gap between geotechnical hazard and structural performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating high-resolution site characterization with advanced nonlinear soil-structure interaction (SSI) analysis will reveal that lateral spreading displacement patterns are highly sensitive to local stratigraphy and building foundation type, leading to demands that differ significantly from regional empirical estimates.
proposals.method: We will first compile and analyze a region-specific geotechnical database from existing borehole logs, CPT data, and Vs profiles (leveraging Brandenberg's prior work on Vs databases and site response). Using OpenSeesPL or FLAC, we will develop 2D nonlinear finite difference models of the soil profile at representative sites, calibrated with recorded ground motions. The models will incorporate advanced constitutive models (e.g., PM4Sand) to simulate liquefaction and lateral spreading. Specific masonry and wood frame structures will be modeled as nonlinear beam-column elements with appropriate foundations, and the fully coupled soil-structure models will be subjected to a suite of ground motions to compute displacement demands.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, open-access dataset of coupled soil-structure response for lateral spreading, directly informing fragility model development for these common building types. The results will provide a methodological framework for moving from regional site characterization to structure-specific performance assessment, a critical step for risk mitigation in similar communities.
proposals.novelty: While lateral spreading modeling exists, the explicit, high-fidelity coupling of detailed geotechnical models of Trona/Argus soils with nonlinear structural models of the specific, prevalent building typologies in those towns represents a novel, site- and structure-specific advancement beyond generic empirical correlations.

proposals.title: A Dynamic, Data-Assimilative Regional Model for Liquefaction Susceptibility in the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta Using Ground-Motion Sequences and Geospatial Databases
proposals.motivation: Regional liquefaction susceptibility models are typically static, based on pre-event geotechnical data, and do not update with observed seismic performance. The 2023-2024 earthquake sequences in California highlight the need for models that can assimilate new ground motion and site response data to dynamically refine susceptibility estimates. This is critical for infrastructure resilience and post-event response in regions like the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta, with its complex, saturated soils.
proposals.hypothesis: Liquefaction susceptibility at a regional scale can be dynamically updated by assimilating ground-motion recordings and inferred site response from earthquake sequences into a geospatial database, leading to a more accurate and time-varying model than static geotechnical maps.
proposals.method: We will leverage the DesignSafe-CI cyberinfrastructure to integrate a regional geospatial database of Vs30, CPT, and SPT data. For each new earthquake in a sequence, we will apply a standardized processing workflow (using a tool like the High-Pass Corner-Frequency Review Tool) to compute site-specific amplification factors and residual ground motions. These will be used to update a Bayesian susceptibility model, where prior probabilities from simplified stress-based methods are updated with likelihoods derived from the observed ground motion intensity and inferred soil behavior. The model will be validated against liquefaction manifestations from recent events.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first open, dynamically updatable regional liquefaction susceptibility model for a high-risk area, hosted on DesignSafe. It will provide a framework for integrating ground-motion processing, site response analysis, and geospatial data assimilation into near-real-time hazard assessment, directly benefiting engineering practice and emergency management.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving from static susceptibility maps to a dynamic, data-assimilative model that explicitly uses ground-motion sequences and processed site-response observations to update regional probabilities, a concept not yet operationalized in pre-2026 practice.

proposals.title: Inversion of Ground Shaking Intensity from Post-Earthquake Liquefaction Manifestations Using Bayesian Inference and High-Resolution Geospatial Data
proposals.motivation: Quantifying historical and paleo-earthquake shaking intensity is critical for seismic hazard analysis, but direct ground-motion records are often unavailable. Liquefaction surface features provide a spatially dense, permanent record of strong shaking. Developing a robust inverse method to estimate shaking intensity from these features would unlock valuable constraints for events where instrumental data is sparse, improving our understanding of ground-motion variability and attenuation in regions like the Central Valley.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial distribution and severity of post-earthquake liquefaction manifestations (e.g., sand boils, lateral spreads) can be used within a probabilistic framework to inversely estimate the peak ground acceleration (PGA) and shaking intensity field that caused them, accounting for site-specific geotechnical conditions.
proposals.method: We will compile a database of liquefaction observations from well-documented events (e.g., 2011 Christchurch, 1989 Loma Prieta) using DesignSafe and other platforms. For each site, we will extract pre-event geotechnical parameters (e.g., Vs30, groundwater depth) from regional databases. The inverse problem will be framed in a Bayesian hierarchical model. The forward model will employ established liquefaction triggering procedures (e.g., Boulanger & Idriss, 2014). Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling will be used to infer the posterior distribution of input shaking intensities across the region that best matches the observed pattern of liquefaction.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, open-source methodology for converting geological/geotechnical field evidence into quantitative ground-motion estimates. It will provide new, spatially distributed intensity data for calibrating ground-motion models and site response in alluvial and deltaic environments. The resulting tool will be valuable for paleoseismology and forensic earthquake engineering.
proposals.novelty: While forward prediction of liquefaction from shaking is standard, the explicit inversion for a shaking field from observed liquefaction patterns is a non-trivial, underdeveloped inverse problem. The integration of high-resolution geospatial data, a regional database approach, and Bayesian inference to solve this geotechnical inverse problem represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Inverting the Site-Response Paradigm: A Machine Learning Framework to Predict Geomorphic Province from Depth-Dependent Site Characteristics
proposals.motivation: Current seismic site response analysis and ground motion models (GMMs) condition site amplification on geomorphic province, using province as a proxy for regional geology and basin structure. This approach assumes province is a known, static input. However, for sites with limited geological context or in complex transition zones, province assignment can be ambiguous, potentially introducing bias into site response estimates and hazard calculations. A method to infer province from measurable site characteristics would improve the robustness of site classification in data-sparse regions.
proposals.hypothesis: Geomorphic province imparts a distinct, recoverable signature on the depth-dependent shear-wave velocity (Vs) profile and associated site response metrics. Therefore, a machine learning model can predict geomorphic province type using these depth-dependent features as input, effectively inverting the traditional conditioning relationship.
proposals.method: We will compile a large, curated database of Vs profiles and associated metadata, leveraging resources like DesignSafe and regional databases (e.g., for the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta). Input features will include depth-binned Vs values, time-averaged Vs to 30m (Vs30), depth to impedance contrasts, and computed site response characteristics (e.g., amplitude and period of peak amplification). We will train supervised classification models, including Random Forests and Gradient Boosting Machines, to predict discrete geomorphic province labels (e.g., Basin, Valley, Mountain). Model performance will be evaluated using cross-validation and tested on held-out regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, data-driven tool for probabilistic geomorphic province classification, reducing subjectivity in site characterization. It will test the fundamental link between province and site response, potentially identifying which depth-dependent features are most diagnostic. The resulting model could be integrated into site investigation workflows and inform the development of next-generation GMMs that treat province as a latent variable.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard direction of inference in site response analysis, treating geomorphic province as an output to be predicted from site data rather than a primary input. While previous work has correlated site response with province, explicitly building a predictive classifier for province from detailed Vs profile data is a novel, inverse approach.

proposals.title: A Non-Ergodic Ground-Motion Model for the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta: A Case for Location-Specific Basin Response from Inception
proposals.motivation: Current ergodic ground-motion models (GMMs) average site effects across diverse regions, potentially misrepresenting the unique, complex response of deep sedimentary basins like the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta. This can lead to significant uncertainty in probabilistic seismic hazard analysis (PSHA) for critical infrastructure in these regions. A shift towards a fully non-ergodic, location-specific framework is needed to capture the persistent, path-dependent basin amplification and resonance effects that define such geologies.
proposals.hypothesis: The seismic response of the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta basin is sufficiently unique and spatially coherent that a fully non-ergodic GMM, developed exclusively from region-specific data, will provide more accurate and lower-variance predictions of ground motion than ergodic or partially non-ergodic models.
proposals.method: We will leverage the extensive regional Vs30 database and ground-motion recordings from the Delta, curated via DesignSafe, to develop a location-specific GMM. The method involves: 1) Implementing a mixed-effects regression with spatially varying coefficients for path and site terms, anchored to Delta-specific attenuation, using a regionalized random effects algorithm. 2) Systematically processing recorded motions with a high-pass corner-frequency selection tool tailored for basin-generated long-period waves. 3) Validating the model against recorded basin amplification from significant regional events, isolating the persistent location-specific component of site response.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce one of the first fully non-ergodic GMMs for a major U.S. sedimentary basin, providing a validated framework for location-specific hazard assessment. It will directly quantify the reduction in aleatory variability achievable by abandoning the ergodic assumption for basin sites, informing future building code provisions and risk models for deltaic regions.
proposals.novelty: The proposal challenges the standard incremental approach to non-ergodic modeling by arguing that for well-instrumented, geologically distinct basins, the ergodic framework should be abandoned from the start, not just adjusted. It uniquely integrates high-resolution Vs30 mapping, advanced ground-motion processing, and spatial regression within a single, basin-focused paradigm.

proposals.title: Spatial Interpolation of Site Amplification Residuals Using Geostatistical Kriging for Improved Regional Ground-Motion Prediction in the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta
proposals.motivation: Current ground-motion models (GMMs) and site response analyses often treat site amplification factors as independent across space, ignoring potential spatial correlation in the residuals. This simplification can lead to an underestimation of uncertainty and bias in regional seismic hazard assessments, particularly in complex geologic regions like the Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta. Accurately modeling these correlated residuals is crucial for improving the reliability of probabilistic seismic hazard analysis (PSHA) and infrastructure design.
proposals.hypothesis: The residuals from site amplification models, derived from region-specific Vs30 data and ground-motion databases, exhibit significant spatial correlation that can be robustly modeled using geostatistical kriging. Incorporating this spatial correlation structure will significantly improve the prediction of site amplification and reduce uncertainty in regional ground-motion estimates.
proposals.method: We will compute site amplification residuals by comparing observed ground motions from the NGA-West2 and DesignSafe databases with predictions from established GMMs and site response analyses. Using the high-density Vs30 database from the Delta region, we will perform empirical semivariogram analysis to quantify the spatial structure of these residuals. Ordinary kriging and universal kriging will be implemented for spatial interpolation across the region, with cross-validation used to optimize model parameters and assess prediction accuracy against held-out data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a spatially continuous model of site amplification residuals for the Delta, directly informing regional PSHA. It will provide a methodological framework for integrating spatial correlation of site effects into hazard calculations, moving beyond the common assumption of spatially independent site terms. The results will be disseminated via the DesignSafe platform to enable open science and replication in other regions.
proposals.novelty: While kriging is established in geostatistics, its direct application to model the spatial correlation of site amplification residuals from ground-motion databases is novel. This approach integrates the researcher's expertise in regional site characterization, ground-motion processing, and open data infrastructure to address a key gap in accounting for spatial dependence in site response uncertainty.

proposals.title: Quantifying Site Response in Alluvial Valleys: A 3D Waveform Modeling Framework Adapted from Seismic Basin Analysis
proposals.motivation: Current site response models for seismic hazard, particularly in regions like California's Central Valley, often rely on simplified 1D assumptions. This fails to capture the complex 3D wavefield effects—such as basin-edge focusing and generation of surface waves—that are well-documented in deep sedimentary basins but are poorly constrained for the shallower, broader geomorphic province of valleys. A systematic framework to model these effects in valleys is needed to reduce uncertainty in ground-motion prediction for critical infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: Advanced 3D waveform modeling techniques, validated for deep sedimentary basins, can be successfully adapted and simplified to characterize and predict frequency-dependent amplification and duration lengthening in alluvial valleys, with the primary controls being valley geometry and shallow shear-wave velocity structure.
proposals.method: We will develop a modular workflow using the open-source computational platform SPECFEM3D for 3D viscoelastic wave propagation. The method begins with constructing a parameterized 3D geologic model of a representative valley (e.g., Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta) using regional Vs30 databases and borehole data. We will implement a suite of synthetic earthquakes across a range of magnitudes and distances. Key analysis will use the high-pass corner-frequency review tool from Brandenberg's prior work to ensure consistent processing, followed by quantification of amplification and duration relative to a reference rock site.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, open-source methodology for 3D valley response analysis, directly contributing to improved site terms in ground-motion models. It will generate a publicly available database of synthetic valley response simulations on DesignSafe, enabling future region-specific studies and serving as a benchmark for evaluating simpler 1D/2D approaches.
proposals.novelty: While 3D basin modeling is established, its deliberate adaptation and simplification for the distinct scale and geology of valleys—a common but under-studied geomorphic province—is a novel synthesis. It strategically transfers high-complexity methods to a problem where their computational cost is newly justifiable given the limitations of current practice.

proposals.title: A Global Predictive Model for Seismic Amplification in Sedimentary Basins: Integrating Basin Geometry, Velocity Structure, and Ground-Motion Databases
proposals.motivation: Sedimentary basins significantly amplify and prolong earthquake ground motions, yet current site-response models are region-specific, limiting their applicability for global seismic hazard assessment and ground-motion prediction. The lack of a globally consistent framework for basin amplification introduces large uncertainties in seismic risk evaluation for major population centers situated on basins worldwide. This project addresses a critical gap in pre-2026 seismic hazard analysis by scaling empirical and physics-based understanding to a global context.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a globally applicable predictive model for basin amplification can be developed by relating fundamental, measurable basin attributes—such as depth to bedrock, shear-wave velocity profile, and 3D geometry—to amplification functions derived from a worldwide database of recorded ground motions.
proposals.method: We will compile a global database of ground-motion recordings and basin metadata, leveraging platforms like DesignSafe for data sharing and high-performance computing. For each basin region, we will extract key parameters (e.g., depth from global Vs30 and sediment thickness maps, basin resonance frequencies) and compute empirical amplification functions using a standardized processing workflow, informed by Brandenberg's prior work on high-pass corner-frequency selection. We will then employ advanced regression techniques and machine learning (e.g., random forests or neural networks) to develop the predictive model, explicitly testing the predictive power of different basin descriptors and their interaction with earthquake source and path effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first open-access, globally calibrated model for predicting basin amplification, directly usable in probabilistic seismic hazard analysis (PSHA) and ground-motion modeling. It will quantify the relative importance of different basin properties on amplification, providing a physical basis for extrapolation to data-scarce regions. The resulting database and model will be a foundational resource for the international earthquake engineering community.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic, global integration of disparate basin datasets with a massive, curated ground-motion database to move beyond region-specific correlations. The application of machine learning to discover scalable, physically interpretable relationships between basin attributes and amplification across diverse tectonic settings represents a significant advance over existing piecemeal approaches.

proposals.title: Inversion of High-Frequency Site Amplification for Depth to a Critical Shear-Wave Velocity Horizon Using Geomorphic Province Constraints
proposals.motivation: Current ground-motion models and site response analyses rely heavily on VS30 as a first-order site parameter, but it fails to capture resonant effects controlled by deeper impedance contrasts. Estimating the depth to a critical velocity horizon (e.g., Vs = 1000 m/s) typically requires expensive subsurface investigations. An inverse method to estimate this depth from readily available amplification data and geomorphic context would significantly improve site characterization where direct measurements are sparse, particularly in regions like the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta with complex basin-edge effects.
proposals.hypothesis: The depth to a fundamental velocity isosurface (e.g., Vs = 1000 m/s) can be reliably estimated by inverting high-frequency (>1 Hz) amplification functions, given known VS30 and the constraints on typical velocity profiles provided by a geomorphic province classification.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian inversion framework. The forward model will use a 1D linear viscoelastic site response code to generate amplification functions for a large suite of synthetic velocity profiles, parameterized by VS30, depth to the target isosurface (Z1000), and profile shape priors defined by geomorphic province (e.g., using the Wills and Clahan 2006 California database). The inverse problem will use observed amplification from the NGA-West2 site database and DesignSafe, along with the province and VS30, to compute a posterior probability distribution for Z1000. The high-pass corner-frequency review tool from Brandenberg's prior work will ensure robust amplification estimates for inversion.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a practical, open-source tool for estimating a key depth parameter from surface wave data or recorded ground motions. It will advance the field by formally integrating geomorphic province information into a probabilistic inversion, moving beyond generic VS30-scaling. The resulting depth estimates will directly improve ground-motion prediction and seismic hazard analysis by better constraining basin depth effects.
proposals.novelty: While forward modeling of amplification from known depth is standard, the explicit inverse problem—estimating depth from amplification constrained by province-specific profile statistics—is a novel synthesis of empirical site classification and inversion theory. This approach leverages the growing database of site-specific amplification measurements in a new, geotechnically constrained way.

ideas.title: Develop a regionally-calibrated global framework for seismic liquefaction capacity prediction
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the expansion of a global liquefaction case history database with the validation of a regionally-derived capacity correlation model to create a novel, tiered predictive framework. The expanded global dataset will be used to test the Japanese model's transferability and to identify key geotechnical, seismological, and geological parameters that cause regional deviations in performance. The outcome will be a unified framework that applies a base model calibrated on high-quality global data, with region-specific adjustment factors or modules derived from the systematic analysis of residuals. This approach moves past binary model validation to provide a scalable, context-aware tool for liquefaction hazard assessment worldwide.

ideas.title: Cross-Regional Validation of Seismic Capacity Model Using Ecological Niche Modeling Techniques
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Ecology, specifically the validation techniques used in Species Distribution Modeling (SDM). In SDM, a model calibrated on species occurrence data from one region is tested for transferability by applying it to a different geographic region with analogous environmental conditions (the 'niche space'). The analogous solution involves defining the multi-dimensional niche (e.g., temperature, precipitation, soil pH), identifying regions with comparable niches, and rigorously testing the model's predictive power there, accounting for local idiosyncrasies. Adapted to the seismic capacity correlation model, the research would first define the model's 'niche' using the key parameters from the Japanese case histories (e.g., spectral acceleration at fundamental period, soil type, building material, age, height). It would then source case history databases from other seismically active regions (e.g., the PEER NGA database for California, databases from Europe or New Zealand) and filter them to find subsets that occupy a comparable multi-parameter space to the Japanese calibration set. The model's predictions for capacity (e.g., drift capacity at collapse) would be tested against the observed performance from these matched case histories. The outcome would be a formal assessment of the model's regional transferability, identifying any systematic biases and parameters that may need regional adjustment factors, thereby strengthening the model's global applicability.

ideas.title: Modeling Liquefaction Susceptibility as a Dynamic Ecological Succession
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption of static site conditions by drawing an analogy from ecology. In ecology, systems are understood through succession models, where the current state of a community is dynamically shaped by its history of disturbances and environmental changes. Adapting this solution, we propose to model a site's geotechnical state for liquefaction susceptibility not as a fixed condition but as an evolving one. A state-and-transition framework would be developed where pre-earthquake activities (e.g., groundwater level changes, land reclamation, agricultural practices) and natural processes (e.g., deposition, weathering) act as drivers that transition the soil profile from one 'state' to another, each with a quantifiable liquefaction resistance. This allows for time-variant hazard maps and risk assessments that account for the site's operational history.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Liquefaction Hazard Mapping via Physics-Informed Ensemble Modeling of Subsurface Uncertainty
ideas.description: This adversarially improved research idea develops a hybrid framework that uses surface geology and geophysical data not for direct prediction, but to generate an ensemble of statistically plausible subsurface models. Each model in the ensemble honors physical constraints (e.g., soil mechanics, wave propagation) and is evaluated using standard liquefaction engineering procedures. The system outputs a spatially explicit probabilistic hazard map, quantifying the likelihood and severity of liquefaction while clearly delineating zones of high predictive uncertainty. This approach explicitly acknowledges and manages the limitations of surface data, providing a practical tool for prioritizing where costly and invasive detailed site investigations will have the greatest risk-reduction value.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Reconstruction of Dynamic Site Conditioning for Time-Variant Liquefaction Hazard
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to probabilistically reconstruct the time-varying geotechnical conditions of a site by assimilating heterogeneous, multi-temporal data streams. The system uses a physics-informed neural network or ensemble Kalman filter to fuse direct subsurface snapshots with continuous proxy data (e.g., decades of InSAR, well records, and satellite-based land-use history) to infer the evolution of key parameters like relative density and fines content. This generates an ensemble of plausible subsurface histories, enabling the quantification of how liquefaction susceptibility has changed over time and the associated epistemic uncertainty. The output is a time-variant probabilistic liquefaction hazard model that explicitly accounts for the legacy of both natural processes and human activity.

ideas.title: A 'Negative Data' Registry for Geotechnical Engineering: A Dual Database of Non-Liquefaction Cases
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from pharmaceutical development, where 'negative data' from failed clinical trials is systematically compiled to prevent bias and inform future research. The analogous problem in geotechnical engineering is the publication bias towards case histories where liquefaction occurred, leaving a critical gap in understanding the conditions that prevent it. The adapted solution is to create a dual, 'negative data' registry for non-liquefaction cases. This involves establishing a standardized framework for reporting geotechnical, geological, and seismic parameters at sites that experienced strong shaking but did not liquefy, making this data publicly and systematically available. This registry will directly improve liquefaction susceptibility models by providing the essential counterfactual data needed to define their limits of applicability, reduce redundant site investigations, and foster a more complete understanding of seismic soil performance.

ideas.title: Calibrating Lab-Scale Geotechnical Tests with Field Databases via Climate Model Downscaling Analogy
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from climate science, where Global Climate Models (GCMs) are downscaled using historical data to create validated, high-resolution regional models. The analogous problem in geotechnics is the scale gap between field-scale performance and laboratory element tests. The solution is to use the database of field cases as the calibration and validation dataset. By applying statistical/machine learning downscaling techniques, we can systematically extract the key material states, stress paths, and boundary conditions that govern field failures. This allows for the design of laboratory testing programs where the element tests are not merely geometrically scaled replicas but are fundamentally calibrated simulations that reliably reproduce the essential physics of full-scale field behavior, thereby validating predictive models.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed Remote Liquefaction Detection Validated via a Global Digital Twin Prior
ideas.description: This research develops a method to reliably identify liquefaction using only remote sensing and instrumental data by solving the core validation flaw. It constructs a pre-event 'digital ground truth' model of susceptible sediments from global geological and geotechnical databases fused with high-resolution satellite imagery. A physics-informed machine learning model is trained on a global historical dataset of co-registered remote sensing signals and *legacy* field reconnaissance reports from past earthquakes. For new events, the system applies this validated model to post-event data, using the digital prior to constrain interpretations, thereby achieving robust detection without new field visits.

ideas.title: Dynamic, Asset-Specific Liquefaction Fragility Models Under Non-Stationary Geotechnical and Hydrological Conditions
ideas.description: This research synthesizes high-resolution, asset-specific liquefaction fragility modeling with the concept of non-stationary demand and capacity statistics. It develops dynamic fragility functions for individual structures or infrastructure components (e.g., a specific bridge pier or pipeline segment) where both the seismic demand (ground motion) and geotechnical capacity (soil resistance to liquefaction) are modeled as non-stationary processes influenced by environmental changes, such as rising groundwater, soil consolidation, or climate-driven precipitation patterns. The core innovation is a time-dependent probabilistic model that forecasts how the failure probability of a specific asset evolves, enabling lifecycle risk assessments and proactive, targeted mitigation strategies based on future vulnerability rather than past averages.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Subset Simulation with Version-Controlled Geotechnical Model Lineage
ideas.description: This research fuses subset simulation for low failure probabilities with data versioning principles to create a framework for geotechnical reliability analysis with full model and data lineage. The core idea is to apply Git-like version control not just to static databases, but to the entire probabilistic workflow—tracking changes to input parameters, subsurface models, simulation codes, and resulting intermediate subsets. This creates an auditable trail from raw data revisions through each stage of the subset simulation, allowing analysts to precisely quantify how updates in data or model assumptions propagate to changes in the computed failure probability. The synergy provides a novel capability for explainable and reproducible risk assessment, where the sensitivity of low-probability estimates to epistemic uncertainties in data curation and model evolution can be systematically traced and understood.

ideas.title: Interactive Web-GIS for Regional Liquefaction Susceptibility Modeling and Scenario Exploration
ideas.description: This research develops a web-based GIS platform that integrates predictive models for liquefaction susceptibility which require only surface geology and common geophysical data. The platform's advanced visualization features allow users to interactively run scenarios, adjust model parameters, and query the resulting hazard maps to understand spatial patterns and uncertainties. This enables planners and engineers to rapidly assess regional risk without detailed site investigations, while the interactive interface makes the model's assumptions and limitations transparent. The synergy lies in using sophisticated visualization not just to display results, but to actively explore and validate the simplified models, fostering trust and informed decision-making in data-scarce environments.

ideas.title: Adapting Exoplanet Atmospheric Detection to Identify Soil Liquefaction from Remote Data
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that field reconnaissance is necessary to identify soil liquefaction by drawing an analogy from exoplanet science. In that field, astronomers remotely detect atmospheric composition by analyzing spectroscopic changes in starlight during planetary transits, never physically sampling the target. The adapted solution applies this logic to geotechnical engineering: develop methods to identify liquefaction by analyzing pre- and post-event multi-spectral and hyperspectral remote sensing data to detect the unique spectral signature of liquefied soil (e.g., combined indicators of high surface moisture, sediment redistribution, and altered texture). This spectral fingerprint would be calibrated and validated against co-located instrumental data (seismic records, InSAR ground deformation, pore pressure models) from the triggering earthquake, creating a purely remote and instrumental detection framework.

ideas.title: Validating Non-Gaussian Reliability Models via High-Fidelity Laboratory Testing Informed by Field Data
ideas.description: This research synthesizes field-informed laboratory testing with advanced reliability methods by using high-fidelity, database-calibrated element tests to physically validate non-Gaussian correlation functions used in level-crossing reliability analyses. Laboratory programs are designed to replicate the specific loading and environmental sequences extracted from field case databases, creating controlled experiments whose outputs can be directly compared to the predictions of different theoretical correlation models. This allows for the empirical benchmarking of various non-Gaussian models against physical failure data, moving beyond purely mathematical comparison. Consequently, the approach establishes a rigorous, data-driven pathway to select and calibrate the most appropriate correlation functions for predicting real-world structural or geotechnical system reliability.

ideas.title: Crowdsourced Spatiotemporal Monitoring of Levee Degradation via Surface Manifestations
ideas.description: This research fuses time-dependent levee degradation models with citizen science data collection to create a dynamic, evidence-based monitoring framework. Volunteers on platforms like Zooniverse are trained to identify and tag temporal sequences of surface evidence (e.g., evolving sand boils, vegetation changes, cracks) from archival and recent satellite/drone imagery of levee networks. These crowdsourced spatiotemporal datasets are then used to inform, calibrate, and validate the parameters of physical or empirical models for internal erosion, piping, and settlement that drive capacity loss over time. The synergy enables a feedback loop where model predictions guide targeted citizen observation campaigns, and the collected visual evidence continuously updates the reliability analysis, moving from static assessment to a conditionally updated forecast of levee segment performance.

ideas.title: Asset-Specific Fragility via Pharmacological Dose-Response Modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts the dose-response modeling approach from pharmacology to develop asset-specific liquefaction fragility functions. In pharmacology, dose-response curves are tailored to specific patient sub-populations using clinical data and covariates. Analogously, we propose using the liquefaction case history database to develop fragility functions where ground motion intensity is the 'dose' and damage state is the 'response.' The solution involves statistically fitting functions that model the probability of damage as a function of shaking intensity, with the function's parameters directly dependent on the specific characteristics of an individual asset or infrastructure component (e.g., foundation type, embedment depth, surrounding soil density), thereby scaling down from class-averaged to asset-specific fragility predictions.

The core synergy is the creation of automated verification scripts specifically designed for the unique challenges of InSAR data, such as phase unwrapping errors, atmospheric artifacts, and reference frame consistency. This transforms the upload process from a simple data transfer into an active quality control and early anomaly detection system, significantly enhancing the reliability of the integrated remote sensing data for downstream analysis and decision support in geohazard assessment.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed Bayesian Hierarchical Modeling of Levee Capacity Using Sparse Direct Measurements and Dense Proxy Data
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid modeling framework that overcomes the critical data scarcity problem for direct levee capacity assessment. It integrates a physics-based model (e.g., for slope stability or seepage) informed by densely available geospatial proxy data within a Bayesian hierarchical structure. The model is calibrated and validated using the limited, expensive direct measurements, allowing it to 'learn' the relationship between proxies and true capacity. The output is a spatially continuous probabilistic map of levee capacity with quantified uncertainty, enabling robust risk management without requiring impractical levels of direct testing.

ideas.title: Adapting Higher-Order Spectral Methods from Ocean Wave Statistics to Non-Gaussian Level-Crossing Reliability
ideas.description: The classical level-crossing reliability method (Rice's formula) assumes Gaussian processes characterized entirely by their power spectral density (second-order correlation). When structural responses are non-Gaussian—due to nonlinear material behavior, non-Gaussian loading, or geometric nonlinearity—these predictions become inaccurate. An analogous challenge was solved in ocean engineering, where predicting extreme wave crests in nonlinear sea states demanded going beyond the Gaussian Rayleigh distribution. Ocean engineers incorporated bispectral and trispectral analyses (third- and fourth-order correlation functions) from second-order nonlinear wave theory to capture the skewness and kurtosis of wave elevation, producing corrected exceedance probability formulas (e.g., Tayfun and Forristall distributions). Adapting this approach, the proposed research would: (1) characterize non-Gaussian structural response processes via their bispectrum and trispectrum, computed from nonlinear finite-element simulations or measured data; (2) derive modified level-crossing rate expressions that incorporate these higher-order spectral moments as correction terms to Rice's formula; and (3) benchmark the higher-order spectral crossing-rate predictions against Monte Carlo simulation and existing moment-based non-Gaussian crossing methods (e.g., Hermite moment model, saddle-point approximation) across several structural reliability problems. This spectral-domain analogy offers a systematic, physically grounded framework for integrating non-Gaussian correlation structure into crossing-rate reliability without requiring full probabilistic transformation of the response process.

ideas.title: A Blockchain-Verified, Crowdsourced Global Database of Non-Liquefaction Case Histories
ideas.description: This research proposes the development of a decentralized, global database for non-liquefaction case histories, where each site investigation, field test result, and seismic observation is submitted as a transaction to a permissioned blockchain. This creates an immutable, timestamped audit trail for every data entry, ensuring its provenance and preventing tampering or selective omission. The system incentivizes researchers and practitioners to contribute both positive and negative performance data by providing cryptographic proof of their contribution and the data's integrity. The result is a high-fidelity, trusted resource that significantly improves seismic performance models by providing verifiable, non-liquefaction evidence that is currently underrepresented in the literature.

ideas.title: Relax Gaussian process assumption for limit state using spatial statistics and machine learning priors
ideas.description: This idea challenges the standard Gaussian process assumption for the spatial limit state function in reliability analysis by drawing an analogy from spatial ecology. In species distribution modeling, analogous problems of non-Gaussian, sparse, and bounded spatial data are addressed using Generalized Linear Mixed Models (GLMMs) with non-Gaussian link functions and flexible spatial random effects (e.g., Gaussian Markov Random Fields via SPDE). The adapted solution proposes to model the spatial limit state function within a similar latent variable framework: a spatially correlated random field with non-Gaussian marginal distribution (e.g., logistic, Gumbel) or driven by non-Gaussian innovations is used as a prior, linked to the observed failure/success data through a probabilistic model. This relaxes the restrictive Gaussian assumption, allowing for more accurate modeling of tail dependencies and spatial failure mechanisms in reliability analysis of structures and systems.

ideas.title: A predictive framework for non-Gaussian correlation selection in level-crossing reliability analysis
ideas.description: This research develops a principled, generative framework to predict when non-Gaussian correlation functions are essential for accurate level-crossing reliability estimates. Instead of a direct comparison, it establishes a taxonomy of non-Gaussian processes tied to physical mechanisms (e.g., material inhomogeneity, nonlinear system responses) and uses this to generate synthetic and benchmark stochastic fields. A core output is a meta-model that maps quantifiable descriptors of a process's non-Gaussianity and correlation structure to the expected error of the Gaussian assumption, providing engineers with a prescriptive tool for correlation function selection. The system is validated on case studies from wind engineering and fatigue crack growth, demonstrating its robustness in moving beyond description to actionable prediction.

ideas.description: This approach develops a hierarchical Bayesian model to estimate liquefaction fragility for individual assets by integrating sparse asset-specific data with the broader patterns in the regional or global database. The model structure defines shared hyper-parameters for groups of similar assets (e.g., by foundation type, construction era, soil class), which inform the priors for the parameters of an asset's unique fragility function. This produces a posterior fragility estimate for the target asset that rigorously quantifies epistemic uncertainty due to data scarcity, resulting in a robust, probabilistic function usable for risk-informed decision-making.

ideas.title: Adapt Copula-Based Spatial Dependence Models from Finance for High-Water Demand Correlation
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the solution to modeling correlated financial risks—specifically the use of copula functions—to the problem of spatial correlation for high-water demands. In finance, copulas are used to model the dependence between assets without relying on simplistic, often inaccurate, assumptions of perfect or Gaussian correlation, which is crucial for stress testing and tail risk assessment. The adapted idea involves developing spatial correlation models for water demand nodes (e.g., municipalities, agricultural zones) by employing copula theory to statistically link their marginal demand distributions. This approach will characterize the true likelihood of concurrent high-demand events across a region, moving beyond the common but unrealistic assumption of perfect correlation. The resulting models will enable more robust planning and design of water supply infrastructure by providing a probabilistic understanding of system-wide demand extremes.

ideas.title: Adapting Geological Stratigraphy for Layered Data Provenance on Blockchain
ideas.description: This idea adapts the geological concept of stratigraphy—using layered rock formations to create a chronological history—to the problem of data provenance. In geology, the superposition of strata provides an immutable, ordered record of events. The analogous solution is implemented by structuring a blockchain's audit trail as a series of data 'strata.' Each block is a stratum containing a cryptographic hash of the data's state at that time, along with contextual metadata. This creates a clear, immutable, and visually interpretable lineage where the sequence and content of layers explicitly show the entire history of submissions and modifications, significantly enhancing trust and auditability in data-centric workflows.

ideas.title: Adapting sequential Monte Carlo rare-event sampling from nuclear reactor safety to levee failure probability estimation
ideas.description: Nuclear reactor probabilistic risk assessment (PRA) faced a nearly identical computational problem: estimating extremely low failure probabilities (1e-6 to 1e-8) for complex multi-component systems under uncertainty. The nuclear community solved this through multilevel splitting Monte Carlo combined with structured fault tree / event tree decomposition, where rare catastrophic outcomes are decomposed into sequences of progressively more severe intermediate states. Samples surviving each intermediate threshold are split and propagated forward, concentrating computational effort on failure-relevant regions of the input space. Critically, nuclear PRA also developed importance measures (Fussell-Vesely importance, Risk Achievement Worth, Risk Reduction Worth) that quantify each component's contribution to system failure probability. Adapting this to levee reliability, we propose defining intermediate failure surfaces along the physical breach progression—seepage initiation through the levee body, advancement of internal erosion piping, slope instability development, and full structural breach—and applying subset simulation across these physically meaningful thresholds rather than arbitrary probability levels. The nuclear-inspired importance measures are then computed as byproducts of the simulation to rank which levee segments, soil parameters, or hydraulic boundary conditions are most critical to overall system reliability, directly informing inspection and reinforcement priorities. This yields not only efficient estimation of failure probabilities below 1e-5 but also decision-relevant sensitivity information that pure subset simulation does not natively provide.

ideas.title: Transport-Map Augmented Gaussian Processes for Non-Gaussian Limit State Surrogate Modeling
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the computational intractability of directly modeling non-Gaussian spatial limit state functions by composing a standard Gaussian process surrogate with a learned monotone transport map that warps the GP output distribution to match the true non-Gaussian marginal and dependence structure. The transport map is parameterized as a triangular polynomial or neural network and fitted to a small set of expensive limit state evaluations, preserving the O(n^3) scaling of GP inference while capturing heavy tails, skewness, and multimodal failure regions that pure GP models miss. An active-learning loop operates in the warped space, selecting new training points where the transported posterior uncertainty over the failure boundary is greatest, ensuring sample-efficient refinement. This framework retains the closed-form predictive distributions needed for fast reliability estimation (e.g., FORM/SORM or importance sampling) while providing provably better calibrated failure probability estimates for limit states with strongly non-Gaussian spatial variability.

ideas.title: Hybrid Field Data and Synthetic Failure Simulation for Robust Laboratory Test Design
ideas.description: This research develops a methodology that combines historical field case databases with physics-based numerical simulations to generate a comprehensive set of failure scenarios, including both observed and critical hypothetical cases. Laboratory element testing programs are then designed to explicitly validate models against this expanded scenario set, ensuring tests probe system boundaries and coupled failure modes not captured by historical data alone. The approach uses Bayesian updating to refine simulation parameters based on laboratory results, creating a closed-loop validation framework. This produces more robust laboratory validation protocols that are resilient to the incompleteness and biases inherent in field databases.

ideas.description: This system extends Git-like versioning with a pre-commit validation layer that requires structured, schema-compliant scientific metadata for every change. Contributors must submit a machine-readable manifest detailing provenance, methodology, and uncertainty, which is validated against community-agreed standards before integration. This transforms the database into a curated knowledge graph where the version history captures not only data evolution but also the rigorous scientific context of each change, enabling auditability, reproducibility, and quality filtering directly within the version control paradigm.

ideas.title: Automated, Policy-Driven Data Ingestion and Validation for Heterogeneous Data Lakes

The tools will ingest raw, heterogeneous data from contributors and automatically apply validation rules, extract or assign metadata, and check for compliance with predefined data governance policies before committing files to the lake. This ensures the data lake is populated with well-described, quality-checked assets from the outset, dramatically reducing the 'data swamp' risk. The synergy enables scalable, trustworthy management of diverse data sources by embedding curation into the ingestion process itself.

ideas.title: Dynamic Data Templates for Non-Stationary Risk Inference
ideas.description: This research develops intelligent, standardized data submission templates that are specifically designed to capture the parameters and contextual drivers of non-stationary processes. The templates guide users to report not just static values, but also time-series data, suspected change points, and associated environmental or socio-economic covariates. This structured, rich data feed then directly fuels statistical models that challenge stationarity, allowing for the systematic inference of evolving demand and capacity distributions. The synergy transforms passive data collection into an active tool for learning about temporal dynamics, creating a scalable framework for updating risk assessments in near real-time.
